issue_title: Missing support for multiple types
issue_body:
http://json-schema.org/latest/json-schema-validation.html#anchor79
> The value of this keyword MUST be either a string or an array. If it is an array, elements of the array MUST be strings and MUST be unique.

...
> An instance matches successfully if its primitive type is one of the types defined by keyword.

I take this to mean that I can use
```yaml
properties:
    foo:
        type:
            - 'string'
            - 'null'
```
But Bravado bombs:
> SwaggerMappingError: Don't know how to unmarshal value None with a type of ['string', 'null']

Unfortunately, I can't remove the `null` type here, because I _do_ get strings and nulls in that property from my backend, and validation fails for nulls; non-required properties are expected to _not exist_ in the dictionary and do not accept a `null` value.

issue_title: Unmarshal fails with multi-type additionalProperties
issue_body:
It's quite unusual to have something like this, but given an object:

```yaml
  stored-object:
    type: object
    properties:
      data:
        type: object
        properties:
          id:
            type: string
        additionalProperties:
          type: [integer, string, boolean]
      permissions:
        $ref: '#/definitions/permissions'
```

I've got this error on `pyramid_swagger`:

```json
{
    "code": "400 Bad Request",
    "message": "The server could not comply with the request since it is either malformed or otherwise incorrect.\n\n\nDon't know how to unmarshal value aaa with a type of [u'integer', u'string', u'boolean']\n\n",
    "title": "Bad Request"
}
```

issue_title: Marshall error even if "validate_requests" is False
issue_body:
In bravado v9, this [line](https://github.com/Yelp/bravado-core/blob/master/bravado_core/param.py#L120) is raising a `bravado_core.exception.SwaggerMappingError` in case that the request param type is not satisfied. 

This is totally fine in a common scenario, but in a testing scenario where you want to test multiple input combinations, the typical approach would be disabling `validate_requests` config option, but this does not work, since the exception would be raised anyway.

I think we should change this behavior. If you agree, I can try to provide a PR too.

issue_title: WindowsError on runprofileserver
issue_body:
Hi,

If I run following command 

     python manage.py runprofileserver --kcachegrind --prof-path=..\tmp-profile

and make a request from browser (127.0.0.1:8000), the following output is shown:

    A server error occurred.  Please contact the administrator.

The cmd shows:

    (example) E:\projects\example\example>python manage.py runprofileserver --
    kcachegrind --prof-path=..\tmp-profile
    Validating models...
    System check identified no issues (0 silenced).
    
    Django version 1.7.2, using settings 'example.settings'
    Development server is running at http://127.0.0.1:8000/
    Quit the server with CTRL-BREAK.
    Traceback (most recent call last):
      File "D:\Python27\Lib\wsgiref\handlers.py", line 85, in run
        self.result = application(self.environ, self.start_response)
      File "E:\projects\example\lib\site-packages\django_extensions\management\commands\runprofileserver.py", line 254, in handler
        os.rename(profname, profname2)
    WindowsError: [Error 32] The process cannot access the file because it is being used by another process
    [06/Jan/2015 14:03:32] "GET / HTTP/1.1" 500 59

I have following environment:
Windows 7
Python 2.7.8

>pip freeze
Django==1.7.2
Pillow==2.7.0
PyJWT==0.3.2
django-cors-headers==1.0.0
django-debug-toolbar==1.2.2
django-extensions==1.4.9
django-filter==0.9.1
django-nose==1.3
django-reversion==1.8.5
django-taggit==0.12.2
djangorestframework==3.0.2
djangorestframework-jwt==1.1.1
factory-boy==2.4.1
ipython==2.3.1
mock==1.0.1
nose==1.3.4
pyreadline==2.0
pytz==2014.10
six==1.9.0
sqlparse==0.1.14

The reason seems that in the command runprofileserver.py the file `profname` is not closed:

    if USE_LSPROF:
        kg = KCacheGrind(prof)
        kg.output(open(profname, 'w'))

solution is:

    if USE_LSPROF:
        kg = KCacheGrind(prof)
        with open(profname, 'w') as f:
             kg.output(f)

issue_labels: [{'id': 578, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/django-extensions/django-extensions/labels/feature%20request', 'name': 'feature request', 'color': '4a9481', 'default': False}, {'id': 30635832, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/django-extensions/django-extensions/labels/need%20test%20case', 'name': 'need test case', 'color': 'ededed', 'default': False}, {'id': 4953020, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/django-extensions/django-extensions/labels/patch%20need%20improvement', 'name': 'patch need improvement', 'color': '444444', 'default': False}]
issue_body: when debugging Django rest framework serializers using shell life would be much easier if we can use shell plus to auto-load serializers. is this possible right now ?

issue_title: How do I install Watchman on windows?
issue_body:
Maybe I'm not technical enough to understand the user-guide for installing on windows machine

`Extract the zip file and make sure that watchman.exe is located in a directory that is in your PATH`

The first part I understand zip the file however the second part I don't understand 
**located in a directory that is in your PATH**

What does it mean? Why not just release it as a npm module that you can install cli globally on your machine. Doing stuff manually like this `located in a directory that is in your PATH` does not make any sense it just doesn't. It's not user-friendly

Why I'm asking this is that there are a lot of packages/plugins that rely on this or build on top of it like `GraphQL language service` or the `flow-language-server`.

issue_title: Unexpected behavior in content.delete
issue_body:
If I do:
```
api.content.delete(objects=[])
```
I expect that the function will do nothing and exit without errors. However, an exception is raised. 

The reason is the first line of code of the `delete` function:
```
objects = objects or [obj]
```
Since `objects` is an empty list and `obj` is `None` (the default value) we end up with `objects` being `[None]`.

An easy way to fix is to filter out `None` objects from the resulting list:
```
objects = objects or [obj]
objects = [o for o in objects if o is not None]
if not objects:
    return
```

issue_title: Include protect.js for Plone 4? And Plone 5?
issue_body:
Basically, see https://github.com/plone/plone4.csrffixes/issues/19
And possibly related thread on community.plone.org: https://community.plone.org/t/plone-protect-and-ajax-post-requests/2010

In short:
Plone 4.3.9 has all fixes from plone4.csrffixes included, except the plone.protect pin and the transform which adds `protect.js` and blesses a few read-on-writes. These read-on-writes are presumably already blessed by plone.protect 3.x or have been fixed in their respective packages. But the javascript still seems needed for best experience.

Should we add this script on plone.protect master and perhaps include it only when the Plone version is 4.x? My guess is that adding it to the auto transform would work and is not too invasive.
Or is this something we should do in for example CMFPlone?

The last run had dropout 20% and with the TED data set we had

- Training WER was 6.16% 
- Validation was 37.32%
- Test was 41.02%

The large difference between the training and validation/test WER imply dropout is too low.

Thus this issue's goal is to increase the dropout to 40%. (We have in the past doubled the dropout each time we've found it to be too low.)

issue_title: Improve performance of EDTFField
issue_body:
The `EDTFField` stores parsed EDTF values as text in the underlying database, and converts this stored text value back into a python object when a model instance's data is loaded from the DB.

Unfortunately the conversion of text data into Python does a full pass through the EDTF grammar parsing process, which as noted in #11 is quite slow. This means that it is slow just to populate a model instance using an `EDTFField` from the database, even if that field is not used.

In anecdotal testing I have seen it take from 1/4 to 1/2 a second to load each model instance with a non-empty EDTF field from the database, which can be crippling when performing operations on many items such as listings.

To improve performance the `EDTFField` should probably be changed to stored pre-parsed values – as generated by the `pre_save` method – in the DB in a way that can be very quickly and directly converted back to Python by the `from_db_value` method without needing to be parsed.

issue_title: Parameters aren't validated when passed via constructor
issue_body:
``` python
class Task(luigi.Task):
    p = luigi.IntParameter()

print Task(p='hi').p  # 'hi'
```

While the above is a trivial case of "something will probably break down the line," this can introduce subtle bugs when e.g. a date parameter is passed to a datetime parameter (or vice-versa).

My suggestion would be to add some sort of validation, and possibly the ability to tweak the value (similar to parse) to the Parameter interface. The latter would be useful when implementing an e.g. "every N hours" parameter that automatically floors to the nearest hour.

issue_title: Armorable.from_blob fails if the blob is a bytes object
issue_body:
because it assumes it is a str, and this may not always be the case.

Probably the easiest fix would be to change:

```python
def from_blob(cls, blob):
    obj = cls()
    po = obj.parse(bytearray(blob, 'latin-1'))
```

to

```python
def from_blob(cls, blob):
    obj = cls()
    if isinstance(blob, six.binary_type):
        po = obj.parse(bytearray(blob))
    else:
        po = obj.parse(bytearray(blob, 'latin-1'))    
```

issue_title: boto silently downloads partial files with rare s3 error condition
issue_body:
We recently encountered a rare error condition in S3 where the download of a larger file (2+ Mb) would hang unexpectedly. Somewhere in the network layer the connection would time out with only a portion of the file transmitted. When this happened `get_contents_to_file` would write as much data as was received and then exit with apparent success, leaving the user with truncated files and no indication that an error had occurred. 

We have followed up fairly extensively with S3 business support and although neither we nor they have been able to pin down exactly what's causing the hang, they recommend checking at the end of the download to make sure that the amount of data received matches with what `Content-Length` says it should be. Here's what Amazon support actually said:

> In general, with issues like these, the S3 service team would usually recommend implementing a form of error checking (like you already have with comparing Etags) of somehow comparing the downloaded object's properties with what you're expecting. A simple alternative example of this would be to just compare the retrieved Content-Length of the object to the length of the downloaded object to see if they match. In a perfect world, it would be ideal that intermittent errors like these didn't occur ever, but just the nature of having data traverse a distributed system like S3 entails having to be able to appropriately handle unexpected responses. 

We're currently implementing the check outside of boto; eventually we'll get a pull request together to make it happen inside `get_contents_to_file` and raise an appropriate exception, but in the meantime we felt it was important to get the info out there in case someone else runs into the same problem or someone wants to take a stab at fixing it.

issue_title: Probable memory leak in Kinesis client get_records() during sustained ReadProvisionedThroughputExceeded errors
issue_body: I observed fairly consistent Python heap growth in my service over several weeks. Every couple of days, the heap growth would lead to a soft and then eventually hard failure of my system. The application's main loop polls Kinesis for new records. In monitoring to determine the root cause, I noticed that despite there being zero `ClientError`s raised by `get_records()`, there were more than 3 ReadProvisionedThroughputExceeded errors per second registered on in the CloudWatch metrics for my stream. The cause of the errors was not from my application exceeding directly the allotted 5 reads per second per shard. Rather, they were caused by somehow having multiple Lambda triggers for the same Lambda function registered to the stream being polled. After several days of issues, crashes, and restarts, I noticed the duplicate Lambda trigger registration, and I deleted it. Immediately from that moment since (now over 3 days since), the ReadProvisionedThroughputExceeded errors halted, and the heap growth in my service immediately leveled off. Attached above is a screenshot taken about 1 day after fixing the double trigger. On the top, you can see the green trace of elevated ReadProvisionedThroughputExceeded errors suddenly drop to 0. On the bottom, you can see the blue trace showing the jagged pattern of memory heap growth suddenly stabilize as soon as the double trigger is corrected. I believe that there is a memory leak in Boto3 during periods of elevated ReadProvisionedThroughputExceeded errors on a Kinesis stream. The confusing part is that not a single `ClientError` was raised. I suspect this has to do with the retry logic internal to all of the Boto3 clients, and perhaps its also related to the way that Lambda triggers affect AWS HTTP responses, and how they're handled by Boto3. I don't have definitive proof of the memory leak because it is not easy to reproduce, and because it occurred in my production environment where it's not practical to run Python heap debuggers and other tools. Let me know if there's additional information I can supply related to this issue. I think it's important enough to share with the maintainers here, since the issue was not visible to me as a client and since it appears w.h.p. to be the cause of my application's performance degrading to the point of being out of service.

issue_title: ciso8601 should specify and enforce a code style
issue_body:
The codebase of ciso8601 is a mix of various C coding/formatting styles.

The classic example in C is where opening `{` should go. In ciso8601, we have examples of both:

* [Same line](https://github.com/closeio/ciso8601/blob/e886109dc8f6a7168a1d4d38ca3b48ef26a03610/module.c#L187)
* [Next line](https://github.com/closeio/ciso8601/blob/e886109dc8f6a7168a1d4d38ca3b48ef26a03610/module.c#L145)

`ciso8601` should specify a C formatting style and enforce it. 
This could then be put into a [`.clang-format` file](https://clang.llvm.org/docs/ClangFormatStyleOptions.html), so that developer's IDE's could enforce the style as they code.

While I don't care which format is chosen, I do want ciso8601 to have a consistent style and for my contributions to match that style.

I've got Waitress serving a Django app on Heroku. Recently, something has started to cause the `waitress-serve` process to hang until it is forcibly restarted. The only error I get is the Heroku router timeout error after 30 seconds. Nothing is being reported to New Relic. I guess that the heroku router is passing on the request to the process and getting nothing back aside from an acknowledgment that the request has been registered (I assume?)

Is there anyway to debug what's going on? Some extra logging info that can be gleaned from the processes perhaps?

At the moment I am resorting to automatically restarting the processes once per hour to pre-emtively stop them from crashing. 

Are there any settings or arguments I can use to force a restart after a certain number of requests?

I start it using the following command (I added the cleanup interval and channel timeout after this first started happening, but to no avail)

`waitress-serve --cleanup-interval=20 --channel-timeout=25 --port=$PORT myapp.wsgi:application`

issue_title: Exception triggered when lightweight tag points to blob/tree
issue_body:
# Bug summary
The Reference.TagReference.tag() function in the module git/refs/tag.py in Gitpython 2.1.0 (and potentially other versions after 2.0.8) does not properly handle lightweight tags that point to blobs or trees. 

Below I will demonstrate how to create a tag pointing to a blob or tree. Next I will demonstrate how to trigger the bug in gitpython. Finally, I will show an example popular git repo that contains a tag pointing to a tree. 

I do not have a suggestion for what the correct behavior of Reference.TagReference.tag() should be, but I do not think that it should trigger an exception when the tag points to a blob or tree.

# Sample script to create a repo with a tag pointing to a blob
```
git init foo
cd foo
echo foo > bar.txt
git add bar.txt
git commit -m baz
git tag quux `git hash-object bar.txt`
PAGER=cat git show quux
cd ..
```

# Python script to trigger the bug (using the above repo)
```
# pip install gitpython==2.1.0

import git

tag_name = "quux"
repo_name = "foo"
repo_obj = git.Repo(repo_name)

tag_obj = repo_obj.tags[0]
assert str(tag_obj) == tag_name

if tag_obj.commit.type == 'commit':
    print("Is a commit")
else:
    print("Not a commit")

```
# Traceback from test script
```
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "delme.py", line 10, in <module>
    if tag_obj.commit.type == 'commit':
  File "/usr/lib/python2.7/site-packages/git/refs/tag.py", line 33, in commit
    raise ValueError("Tag %s points to a Blob or Tree - have never seen that before" % self)
ValueError: Tag quux points to a Blob or Tree - have never seen that before
```

# Example from the wild: linux 
Here I will show an example of a popular git repo that includes a tag (v2.6.11) pointing to a blob or tree. Note that Linus Torvalds, the original author of git, probably created this tag, based on the fact that he authored the successively-named tag (v2.6.12-rc2). v2.6.11 does not contain metadata. 

Running these commands:
```
git clone git://git.kernel.org/pub/scm/linux/kernel/git/stable/linux-stable.git linux-stable
cd linux-stable
git show v2.6.11
```
Produces this output:
```
tag v2.6.11-tree

This is the 2.6.11 tree object.

NOTE! There's no commit for this, since it happened before I started with git.
Eventually we'll import some sort of history, and that should tie this tree
object up to a real commit. In the meantime, this acts as an anchor point for
doing diffs etc under git.
-----BEGIN PGP SIGNATURE-----
Version: GnuPG v1.2.4 (GNU/Linux)

iD8DBQBCeV/eF3YsRnbiHLsRAl+SAKCVp8lVXwpUhMEvy8N5jVBd16UCmACeOtP6
KLMHist5yj0sw1E4hDTyQa0=
=/bIK
-----END PGP SIGNATURE-----

tree v2.6.11

COPYING
CREDITS
Documentation/
MAINTAINERS
Makefile
README
REPORTING-BUGS
arch/
crypto/
drivers/
fs/
include/
init/
ipc/
kernel/
lib/
mm/
net/
scripts/
security/
sound/
usr/
```

issue_title: Make Circuits 'Interface Optional'
issue_body:
I understand the intent of wanting an interface attached to a circuit because that's ultimately what the circuit is physically connected to.

But - at least in my environment - the circuit is _really_ tied to the site. If the device (and thus the interface) are replaced for some reason, we don't get a new circuit. It's the same physical/logical circuit with the same properties except for which interface it's connecting to.

I propose that Circuits become a resource that can exist without the creation of the interfaces first; with the interface being optionally attached.

issue_title: Remove SeleniumLibrary profile for Firefox

The change is possibly backwards incompatible and therefore added bwic label.

issue_title: Develop 2018 field and distribute it
SendableChooser fails to get selection when not on field
issue_body:
This year we're using SendableChooser again to select an autonomous mode from the SmartDashboard.  This year in our shop environment (wifi) it is failing to use the selected auto mode.  Oddly, this worked fine at competition last week when connected to the field.  

Looking at the code, it appears that the following call in sendablechooser.py, line 90 in getSelected() is not working as expected:

`table = self.getTable()`

The "getTable" call always returns None, which causes it to use the default mode, regardless of the selection.  If no default mode/object is defined it will throw an exception trying to look up "None" in the map.

This is using pyfrc 2017.1.3, robotpy 2017.1.2, on Windows 10.

issue_title: pipenv install that worked on 9.0.3 fails on 11.1.5
issue_body:
I use pipenv install as part of a CI pipeline. When I updated pipenv version used in the pipeline pipenv install stopped working.

##### Describe your environment

1. OS Type: Linux
1. Python version: `$ python -V`: 3.6.4
1. Pipenv version: `$ pipenv --version` 11.1.5

##### Expected result

Updating pipenv shouldn't make installation stop working. 

##### Actual result

pipenv doesn't install all the dependencies because they have wrong python_requires, for example:

```Ignoring pluggy: markers 'python_version == ">=2.7,!=3.0.*,!=3.1.*,!=3.2.*,!=3.3.*"' don't match your environment```

Please note that despite the version in python_requires all this packages were working before.

##### Steps to replicate

Provide the steps to replicate (which usually at least includes the commands and the Pipfile).

Pipfile:

```
[[source]]

url = "https://pypi.python.org/simple"
verify_ssl = true
name = "pypi"


[packages]

uwsgi = "*"

Command: ``pipenv install --dev --deploy``

issue_title: Document the "upgrade my dependancies" workflow
issue_body:
As far as I can tell, the documentation doesn't seem to lay out a proscribed workflow for upgrading dependencies. I think this is a fairly common use-case, something like this:

> As a user of pipenv, I've got a project with a `Pipfile` and a `Pipfile.lock`. It's been some time, and I'd like to know what's changed upstream, and to upgrade my dependencies (and test them as they go). 

I'm happy to contribute this documentation myself, and I think I've figured out a good workflow. But I'm opening this issue to make sure I'm on the write track before I write something.

Here's what I think the upgrade workflow should look like:

1. Find out what's changed upstream: `pipenv run pip list --outdated`
2. Upgrade packages, two options:
    a. Want to upgrade everything? Just do `pipenv install`.
    b. Want to upgrade packages one-at-a-time? `pipenv install --selective upgrade <pkg>` for each outdated package.
3. Run your tests, fix issues. If there are problems, pin versions in `Pipfile`.
4. Commit your upgraded `Pipfile.lock` (and perhaps `Pipfile`).

Does this sound right?

Something I don't understand about `--selective-upgrade` -- why does it modify `Pipfile` to add `keep_outdated`?

issue_title: Py_DEBUG and WITH_PYMALLOC warnings [Windows]
issue_body:
Hi,

I'm on Windows 7 x64, running the latest Python 3.6.5 (64-bit), with setuptools 39.0.1. Each time a setuptools internal function is invoked (for example by running zc.buildout's [bootstrap.py](https://github.com/buildout/buildout/blob/master/bootstrap/bootstrap.py)) and somehow calls ``pep425tags.get_abi_tag``, 2 warnings relative to ``PyDEBUG`` and ``WITH_PYMALLOC`` are raised. I don't remember seeing these warnings with previous versions of ``setuptools`` (I had 36.x if I recall correctly)

I've tried to search issues relative to these warnings but the only I could find are from 2016 and do not seem to be relevant.

Here is a minimal command that triggers the warnings:

```
$ python -c "from setuptools.pep425tags import get_abi_tag; get_abi_tag()"
C:\Python\3.6\lib\site-packages\setuptools\pep425tags.py:89: RuntimeWarning: Config variable 'Py_DEBUG' is unset, Python ABI tag may be incorrect
  warn=(impl == 'cp')):
C:\Python\3.6\lib\site-packages\setuptools\pep425tags.py:93: RuntimeWarning: Config variable 'WITH_PYMALLOC' is unset, Python ABI tag may be incorrect
  warn=(impl == 'cp')):
```

I am not sure I fully understand what is going on though, so it may have something to do with my setup. Hopefully this can be reproduced somehow.

issue_title: Missing ~/.local/bin/virtualenv after --user installation
issue_body:
**Steps to reproduce:**

* `pip3 install --user virtualenv`
* `pip3 freeze`
* `virtualenv --help`

**What should happen:**

* virtualenv folders are installed in `~/.local/lib/python3.X/site-packages`
* virtualenv binary is installed at `~/.local/bin/virtualenv`
* virtualenv appears in `pip freeze` list
* Can execute `virtualenv` commands from the command line (assuming `~/.local/bin/` is on $PATH)

**What happens instead:**

* virtualenv folders are installed in `~/.local/lib/python3.5/site-packages` (correct)
* `~/.local/bin/` does not contain a file called `virtualenv`
* virtualenv 15.1.0 appears in `pip freeze` list (correct)
* Cannot execute `virtualenv` commands (can execute other commands from `~/.local/bin/`)

**Additional notes:**

* Running Python 3.5.3 on Ubuntu 17.04.
* Running local pip installation: `pip 9.0.1 from ~/.local/lib/python3.5/site-packages (python 3.5)`
* Results from `pip3 uninstall virtualenv` (list of installed files):

```
Uninstalling virtualenv-15.1.0:
  ~/.local/lib/python3.5/site-packages/__pycache__/virtualenv.cpython-35.pyc

  ~/.local/lib/python3.5/site-packages/virtualenv-15.1.0.dist-info/entry_points.txt
  ~/.local/lib/python3.5/site-packages/virtualenv-15.1.0.dist-info/metadata.json
  ~/.local/lib/python3.5/site-packages/virtualenv-15.1.0.dist-info/top_level.txt
  ~/.local/lib/python3.5/site-packages/virtualenv.py
  ~/.local/lib/python3.5/site-packages/virtualenv_support/__init__.py
  ~/.local/lib/python3.5/site-packages/virtualenv_support/__pycache__/__init__.cpython-35.pyc
  ~/.local/lib/python3.5/site-packages/virtualenv_support/argparse-1.4.0-py2.py3-none-any.whl
  ~/.local/lib/python3.5/site-packages/virtualenv_support/pip-9.0.1-py2.py3-none-any.whl

```

This has knock-on effects for tools that depend on virtualenv -- e.g. [pew](https://pypi.python.org/pypi/pew)

**Workaround:**

Install system package (`apt install python3-virtualenv`). Except that I'm trying to create a strictly `--user` environment :)

issue_title: Does this package support Python 3?
issue_body:

issue_body:
DreamHost / DreamObjects supports V4 signatures, but Google Storage in S3-compatible mode doesn't.  The minio-go library supports V4 and V2 signatures to be compatible with Google Cloud Storage, but it seems the Python lib doesn't:
```
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "mintest.py", line 37, in <module>
    for obj in objects:
  File "minio/api.py", line 958, in list_objects
    headers=headers)
  File "minio/api.py", line 1830, in _url_open
    region = self._get_bucket_region(bucket_name)
  File "minio/api.py", line 1764, in _get_bucket_region
    region = self._get_bucket_location(bucket_name)

    raise response_error.get(bucket_name)
minio.error.ResponseError: ResponseError: code: SignatureDoesNotMatch, message: The request signature we calculated does not match the signature you provided. Check your Google secret key and signing method., bucket_name: xxx, object_name: None, request_id: , host_id: , region: 
```
It would be great to  have Google Storage compatibility in the Python version too.

Or, for my use, I don't mind passing an additional arg when creating the client, like sigversion=2 or 4.  I already have a small config file and can add a new field for this.  Since any future S3-compatible storage will more than likely be V4, maybe this is the easiest way.

issue_title: Comments and escaping in plain style
issue_body:
I'm not sure if you're aiming for strict compliance with the YAML spec, but by my reading of the spec ("The plain (unquoted) style has no identifying indicators and provides no form of escaping...Plain scalars must never contain the “: ” and “ #” character combinations."), this should be illegal:

```
- foo:
    my \# commented
    string
```

Yet JS-YAML treats the \ as an escape. Interestingly, it doesn't allow `my \: colon`.

issue_title: Work around incorrect extraction of "reserved" HTML entities
issue_body:
The entities marked as reserved [here](https://do.remifa.so/archives/unicode/latin1.html) (scroll down to see the list) are extracted literally by `lxml`, whereas it should probably strive for more compatibility with browsers which interpret them according to CP1252. A quick example: ```python In [13]: etree.fromstring ('

…

').text Out[13]: u'\x85' ``` whereas modern browsers usually show it as an ellipsis `…`: ```python In [5]: u'\u2026' Out[5]: '…' ```

issue_title: Error with Spider arguments sent using Scrapyd
issue_body:
I figured out the solution to this, but I thought I would put it somewhere for comment and documentation.
I was getting this error when scheduling spider runs through scrapyd with spider arguments.
```
	TypeError: __init__() got an unexpected keyword argument '_job'
```
It's not stated in the documentation explicitly, but when using scrapyd your spiders should allow for any number of arguments which I did not realize. scrapyd was sending a `_job` keyword to the spider, which my spider couldn't handle. So in short your spider `__init__` def should look like
```
    def __init__(self, *args, **kwargs):
```
and look for the keyword of the argument you want instead of
```
    def __init__(self, my_keyword="default"):
```
Hopefully this helps some other python newbies in the future.

issue_title: Pre-releases
issue_body:
We want to make an alpha release of 1.2.
In scrapy, a new release branch is created
and any subsequent PRs for master which are meant for the upcoming release
get merged to both master and the release branch.
(Can be messy when branching too early)

An alternative would be to bump all the pre-releases directly on master
because it's the branch where new development happens.
The amount of PR's with bugfixes is insignificant compared to PRs with features
so the amount of PR's that end up with conflicts because we change their merge base
to something other than the default is less if this default branch is a development branch.
Also, we don't have too many PRs meant for releases later than the upcoming one
so I don't mind keeping them as separate branches and resolving any merge conflicts later.
If they get complex, we can rebase them and merge them in a separate dev branch (eg 1.3dev)
and merge it to master after releasing 1.2.

In the second, more flat, scenario
we are ready to start bumping & tagging 1.2 alphas betas and RCs on master
until we get to gamma and create its dedicated branch.

issue_title: Allow analytics to operate in passthrough mode
issue_body:
Would you be open to a PR that allows the analytics to operate without a write_key?

This would let developers directly integrate analytics in their apps but not actually write data to the API. Definitely useful for debugging and teams where there are variants of dev, staging, test and demo environments, where it's not appropriate to send data to Segment.

Otherwise a developer has to wrap the analytics API in a bunch of boilerplate to not enable/send data.

There's a couple ways that would make it easy to enable this. One would just to add `analytics.passthrough` and change `analytics._proxy` to not actually create the client instance if that is set, perhaps logging the calls instead.

Another could be to make this the default behavior if `analytics.write_key` is `None` or another known, default value. This has the added advantage of allowing late configuration of the `analytics.write_key` setting, which may be necessary in some large, complex applications (e.g. it would get rid of the need for the Django work-around that you have in the docs.)

issue_title: Memory Management of Created Images with Scikit Image
issue_body:
I am working on some trajectory analysis of objects in images using python in a Jupyter notebook. In order to help me understand what is going on in a certain frame (or series of frames) I wrote a function that will create a stack of images that show my trajectories and display them so I can flip through frame by frame to see where the objects are moving similar to [this](http://i.imgur.com/CcuuYYz.png). The problem is that calling the function uses up memory to create the images and display them but that memory is not released or garbage collected after the function ends. This means that if I call the function too many times in my notebook then my OS (Windows) will run out of memory. The only solution I have found so far is to restart the notebook kernel every so often once the python memory limit approaches the maximum in my computer.

I have included a modified version of the function (leaving only the important parts) to show what I am doing. The input is a pandas data frame that contains information about the frame, x position, and y position of each of the objects. I found it easier to use PIL (pillow) to create the images and draw objects on a new image. Finally I use skimage's CollectionViewer which pops open a new window where I can view the image stack and flip through the frames. Then I close the window and go back to doing some analysis in the the Jupyter notebook and call the function again later after some more analysis.

```python
from PIL import Image
from PIL import ImageDraw
import pandas as pd
import skimage

def view_trajectories(data_frame, obj_size=6.0, image_size=(380,380)):
    """Visualize all the trajectories in the data frame

    :param data_frame: the data_frame that contains the trajectory information
    :param obj_size: the size of each object to draw (in pixels)
    :param image_size: Tuple containing the size of the original image
    """

    image_frames = []
    for frame, grp in data_frame.groupby('frame'):
        # Create and new PIL Image Object to draw object on
        img_frame = Image.new('RGB', image_size, 'black')
        img_draw = ImageDraw.Draw(img_frame)

        for idx, obj in grp.iterrows():
            # Draw a circle for a object in each frame
            ellipse_bb = [tuple(obj['x pos':'y pos'].values-obj_size),
                          tuple(obj['x pos':'y pos'].values+obj_size)]
            img_draw.ellipse(ellipse_bb, outline='red')

        # Convert image to array and display it
        image_frame_array = np.array(img_frame)
        img_frame.close()
        image_frame_array = image_frame_array/255.0
        image_frames.append(image_frame_array)

    skimage.viewer.CollectionViewer(image_frames).show()
```
Like I said, ideally I would like to call this function whenever I need to get an idea what the trajectories are doing while I am analyzing them in my Jupyter notebook but release the memory for later use. I have even tried forcing a garbage collect using `gc.collect()`. Is there something I am missing here?

Hi,  
   I am trying to figure out how to deploy fanout/event stream to production.  I have a beta server on elastic beanstalk under https://beta.<mydomainname>.io . It has all the Certificates provisioned by AWS. 

So I've created a production realm in fanout.io for the production version of my site. I created fanout.<mydomainname>.io and now I wanted to clarify the recommended approach. 

Should I essentially have two clusters one for real time with fanout and one for my production. Or do I just put my service behind fanout as a reverse proxy.

I am a bit confused as to how I should set it up. 

 Then the question about SSL comes to mind, is there a pragmatic way for me to refresh my certificates with Fanout using letsencrypt for example. Or do I have to update the certificates manually. 

I couldn't find any deployment instructions in the docs, so I decided to bring this up here, in case others are looking for similar instructions.

issue_title: search: query parser does not parse all possible combinations of NOT operator correctly
issue_body:
The `NOT` operator is not parsed as NotOp.

OR NOT
-----------
`NOT` is parsed as a `Value`.

**Example:**
`test AND (author:"Alexander, S" OR NOT author:"Costa, M S")`

gives

`AndOp(ValueQuery(Value('test')), AndOp(OrOp(KeywordOp(Keyword('author'), DoubleQuotedValue('Alexander, S')), ValueQuery(Value('NOT'))), KeywordOp(Keyword('author'), DoubleQuotedValue('Costa, M S'))))`

AND (NOT ...
-----------------
`NOT` is parsed as a `Value`.

**Example:**
`test AND (NOT author:"Alexander, S")`

gives

`AndOp(ValueQuery(Value('test')), AndOp(ValueQuery(Value('NOT')), KeywordOp(Keyword('author'), DoubleQuotedValue('Alexander, S'))))`

Only AND NOT works
-----------------------------
NOT is then properly parsed as NotOp

**Example:**

`test AND (author:"Alexander, S" AND NOT author:"Costa, M S")`

gives

`AndOp(ValueQuery(Value('test')), AndOp(KeywordOp(Keyword('author'), DoubleQuotedValue('Alexander, S')), NotOp(KeywordOp(Keyword('author'), DoubleQuotedValue('Costa, M S')))))`


If some operations are not supported like `OR NOT`, then server should return an error. It should not parse it sometime as a `Value` and sometime as `NotOp`.

issue_title: WebSession: lighter session management
issue_body:
Originally on 2012-07-03

WebSession: lighter session management

During user storms, session generation may take a long time, making
the site to appear as not available even if there would be resources.
This ticket is about making session management lighter.

(1) The problems occur especially if a site did not run garbage
collection for the session table (`inveniogc -g`) since a long time.
For example, on INSPIRE, the session table once grew to 2+ GB in size.
This and other less-than-optimal DB queries lead to table lock
contention:

```
| 41555 | inspirehep | pcudssw1507.cern.ch:45737 | inspirehep |
          Query | 0 | Locked | INSERT session(
| 41563 | inspirehep | pcudssx1506.cern.ch:33785 | inspirehep |
          Query | 2 | update | INSERT session(
| 41573 | inspirehep | pcudssw1507.cern.ch:45741 | inspirehep |
          Query | 2 | Locked | INSERT session(
| 42667 | inspirehep | pcudssx1506.cern.ch:35370 | inspirehep |
          Query | 1 | Locked | INSERT session(
| 43961 | inspirehep | pcudssw1506.cern.ch:50829 | inspirehep |
          Query | 1 | Locked | INSERT session(
```

The DB was too busy (10+ load average) doing slow queries on other big
tables, so it could not process session queries fast enough because
the session table was also big because garbage collection was not run
there since many moons.  After the slow queries had been fixed and the
table truncated, the response was much faster.

(2) We can make session management lighter by several means, such as:

(2a) Possibly reduce number of SQL queries. Especially for
slashdot-like guest users that only come to a URL because of a tweet.
They are not authorised to anything, they just want to view a picture.

After the session cookie in the browser is created, we don't need to
keep much information, not update session parameters with respect to
session expiry (even for logged in users) much, etc.

If results of a query are not needed immediately, e.g. for session
expiry postponement, consider using `INSERT DELAYED`.  Although this
would tend to accumulate session queries in the DB stack, so this
should only be used for harmless queries indeed.

Technique like that possibly reduce number of SQL queries.

(2b) Switch from MyISAM engine to MEMORY engine.  We don't need to
remember sessions for too long, so they can be held in-memory only.
The memory tables can use B-tree or hash indexes; one can take
advantage of that.  Beware though of `max_heap_table_size` though,
which is something like 256 MB by default; we may need to increase it.

(2c) Switch from MyISAM engine to INNODB engine.  This should reduce
potential table lock contention issues too, since INNODB uses
row-level locking.

(2d) Switch to `memcached` or `redis` for session management.  This
would be very advatageous off-load MySQL fully.  Previous tests
indicated that both `memcached` and `redis` perform about similarly,
so we could take advantage of `redis` that could store session data
lazily onto disk and that could run replicated servers.

(2e) Use different DB hosts to store different sessions, and use
session-sticky haproxy balancing method.  This would be ultimate
session scalability technique for slashdot like scenarios in case we
have tens of web worker nodes.  This is something we can address
later.

(3) These were quick brain-dump notes only without much further
refinement.  We can discuss them IRL.

issue_title: WidgetActionExecutionError
issue_body:
When I have an invariant that launches a [WidgetActionExecutionError](https://github.com/zopefoundation/z3c.form/blob/master/src/z3c/form/interfaces.py#L721), this exception is not catch and is show in the log:

```bash
Traceback (innermost last):
  Module ZPublisher.Publish, line 138, in publish
  Module ZPublisher.mapply, line 77, in mapply
  Module ZPublisher.Publish, line 48, in call_object
  Module plone.app.z3cform.inline_validation, line 29, in __call__
  Module z3c.form.form, line 148, in extractData
  Module z3c.form.field, line 324, in extract
  Module z3c.form.field, line 216, in validate
  Module z3c.form.validator, line 201, in validate
  Module z3c.form.validator, line 206, in validateObject
  Module zope.interface.interface, line 590, in validateInvariants
  Module brasil.gov.integracaocaldav.importagenda, line 38, in validatedatainicialdatafinal
WidgetActionExecutionError
```
This is because the method [validateObject](https://github.com/zopefoundation/z3c.form/blob/master/src/z3c/form/validator.py#L203) only catch exceptions of type [zope.interface.Invalid](https://github.com/zopefoundation/z3c.form/blob/master/src/z3c/form/validator.py#L207) and WidgetActionExecutionError doesn't  inherit from zope.interface.Invalid.

issue_title: Rapidly calling async_send_heap results in segfault
Socket errors are not handled properly which is resulting in unexpected behavior
issue_body:
We are using pymesos in https://github.com/Yelp/task_processing. I ran into an issue where we accepted an offer and launched a task. The elected master was having some issues with networking. Pymesos sent an HTTP request but timed out while getting a response back from master. Master never received the task, thus it did not launch it. socket.timeout error was raised, caught and ignored by pymesos. Our framework assumed that the task has been launched because pymesos did not throw any error. But, it was not actually launched.

Stack trace from our application:
https://gist.github.com/sagar8192/da8f39e04366372815583677dd48c9c5

For POST requests, pymesos should not swallow timeout exceptions. It should raise an exception or return False so that applications know that their request failed.

issue_title: JavaMultipleMethod mismatch for Interface arguments
issue_body:
JavaMultipleMethod resolves to a wrong method signature in a case where matching should be done by the interfaces. Consider the following [Cipher.init](https://docs.oracle.com/javase/7/docs/api/javax/crypto/Cipher.html):

        Cipher = autoclass("javax.crypto.Cipher")
        SecretKeySpec = autoclass("javax.crypto.spec.SecretKeySpec")

        default_key = [0x00] * 16
        secret_key_spec = SecretKeySpec(default_key, "DESede")

        # The signature we want is (int, java.security.Key) [Key is interface]
        # the signature this resolves is (int, java.security.Certificate)
        # JavaException: Invalid instance of u'javax/crypto/spec/SecretKeySpec' passed for a u'java/security/cert/Certificate'
        cipher.init(Cipher.DECRYPT_MODE, secret_key_spec)
 
A (temporary) solution would be manually pull out the correct underlying call signature from `JavaMultipleMethod` object using the interface name in the signature. However is this possible? I did not find any examples doing this and my poking from console did not yield results.

issue_title: "Couldn't load python3.5m: findLibrary returned null" on older versions of Android
issue_body:
When I try to run my APK on older versions of Android (<4.x) I'm getting a crash with this error: "Couldn't load python3.5m: findLibrary returned null"

I'm using the new python-for-android toolchain checked out a few days ago.

I get a popup on the device that says it's an "SDL Error"

logcat: http://pastebin.com/A0EJ8dQG
buildozer: http://pastebin.com/WCbU96TM (I did add python2 to the list of requirements but it did not help)

I also changed the Kivy recipe to use stable as opposed to master. I didn't think that was the problem.

Tested on a Kindle Fire HD (3.x fails), LG Volt (4.4 fails, but for a different reason much later in the startup process after kivy started).

Uninstalling and reinstalling the app did not help.

It looks python-for-android/pythonforandroid/bootstraps/sdl2/build/src/org/kivy/android/PythonUtil.java in the function loadLibraries it is failing to find either python2.7 or python3.5m.

If this all sounds familiar it's a similar problem to #645. In fact before I updated p4a I was at revision 084ddec which @kived had just checked in to fix #645. I was able to build a working version of my app with that revision. After I pulled the latest revision  (9d457e3636d3d55ee2ba781d99faed98c93f5b8f) I immediately started seeing this problem.

issue_title: Unexpected behavior with `match_querystring`
issue_body:
I'm noticing some strange behaviour in 0.6.0 with `match_querystring`. Here is a minimal working example.

In some tests that I write, I want to have URLs that support requests with and without querystring arguments. I was able to do this in 0.5.1, but it seems to not be possible in 0.6.0.

I have limited experience with `responses`, so let me know if this is intended behaviour.

```python
import responses
import requests


@responses.activate
def test_querystring_without_match():
    responses.add(responses.GET, 'http://foo.com', body='bar')
    responses.add(responses.GET, 'http://foo.com?query=foo', body='baz', match_querystring=True)
    print requests.get('http://foo.com').content  # prints 'bar'
    print requests.get('http://foo.com?query=foo').content  # prints 'bar', but I would expect 'baz'


@responses.activate
def test_querystring_with_match():
    responses.add(responses.GET, 'http://foo.com', body='bar', match_querystring=True)
    responses.add(responses.GET, 'http://foo.com?query=foo', body='baz', match_querystring=True)
    print requests.get('http://foo.com').content  # prints 'baz', but I would expect 'bar'
    print requests.get('http://foo.com?query=foo').content  # prints 'baz'

test_querystring_without_match()
test_querystring_with_match()
```

issue_title: User information is missing when using rest_framework
issue_body:
The reason is simple:

https://github.com/getsentry/raven-python/blob/master/raven/contrib/django/client.py#L255

Here raven checks if the request object is of Type Request and rest_framework has its own request object that isn't a subclass of Djangos request object.

Now what do you prefer for a PR to explicitly check for restframework request, or have a duck typing check?

- This causes regressions in the client side

issue_title: Invalid uri characters include | ?
issue_body:
I'm doing work with the BTC2014 dataset and the graph http://didactalia.net/comunidad/materialeducativo/recurso/La-teoria-de-la-relatividad-en-ingles/eb95e40e-937f-4447-a68d-5f1a3fbc97ff has the following URI in it:

http://fonts.googleapis.com/css?family=Nunito|Open%20Sans:300,400,600,700&subset=latin

That led me to the _is_valid_uri function and the list of invalid uri chars, which includes `|`. I can't find anything in the URI spec that says this character is disallowed. I think we should review those characters or find a concrete place where they are disallowed and document that in the exception that's returned.

issue_title: Dev Workflow Suggestion: Online-Checklist for Pull Requests
issue_body:
Hello there,
sorry for the intrusion. GalSim seems to be of very high quality code, and I noticed that you have a nice [checklist on the wiki](https://github.com/GalSim-developers/GalSim/wiki/Checklist-for-making-a-pull-request) to ensure it keeps that way.

Recently I wrote an online-checklist tool exactly for this kind of purpose (code review and pull requests). I thought maybe you liked it and want to use it in your guideline for pull requests?

The nice thing is that it creates a neat "badge" that you can easily paste into github issues and gives the reviewer a warm feeling of comfort. The badges look like this (click it to see the filled-out checklist results):
[![11 Passed, 0 Not Passed, 1 Not Checked](http://www.guess-what.io/badge/esj8y0woB0u8xZ3HYavU7Q.png)](http://www.guess-what.io/c/result/esj8y0woB0u8xZ3HYavU7Q)
Note that I already copied your guidelines into a checklist template for demo purposes here :-)

The tool is completely open source ([github repo](https://github.com/daniel-kun/guess-what)) and free to use.

Greetings,
Daniel

P.S.: Maybe, at a later stage, I might write a GitHub bot that will reply to pull requests without a filled out checklist in them to remind the author to follow the guidelines and paste the checklist results.

issue_title: check_header function should signal what is exactly wrong
issue_body:
I got this error when adding `bin/vsc_env.py` to `vsc-config`:

```
test_importscripts (vsc.install.commontest.CommonTest)
Try to import each python script as a module ... INFO: run_tests from base dir /Users/kehoste/work/vsc-config (using executable /Users/kehoste/work/vsc-config/setup.py)
INFO: generated list: ['bin/vsc_env.py']
INFO: generated scripts list: ['bin/vsc_env.py']
INFO: get_header for script
INFO: python in shebang, forcing env python (header modified)
INFO: run_tests from base dir /Users/kehoste/work/vsc-config (using executable /Users/kehoste/work/vsc-config/setup.py)
INFO: found match url git@github.com:hpcugent/vsc-config in /Users/kehoste/work/vsc-config/.git/config
INFO: found match name vsc-config in /Users/kehoste/work/vsc-config/.git/config
INFO: reg found: ('github.com', 'hpcugent/vsc-config')
INFO: get_name_url returns {'url': 'https://github.com/hpcugent/vsc-config', 'name': 'vsc-config', 'download_url': 'https://github.com/hpcugent/vsc-config/archive/ALL_VERSIONS.tar.gz'}
INFO: found license /Users/kehoste/work/vsc-config/LICENSE with md5sum 4c917d76bb092659fa923f457c72d033
INFO: Found license name ARR and classifier License :: Other/Proprietary License
FAIL
```

The `FAIL` doesn't give any hint on what is wrong exactly, in the end it turned out that the problem was that `/usr/bin/python` was being used as shebang line rather than `/usr/bin/env python`, which is signalled by the `python in shebang` line above.

Problems like this should stand out better so you don't have to be a detective to figure out what the actual problem is.

issue_title: 这个库可以 分片上传？
Support shared secrets instead of passwords in conf file
issue_body:
We need to support a more secure way to supply passwords than putting them in the conf file.  Here's my proposal:

1. support shared secrets configuration, such as Kubernetes secrets
2. add a new key, defaulting to "conf" when not present:

    secrets=[conf,kubernetes,vault,...?]

3. add a new subtree to the config for each secret config, e.g.:

    kubernetes-secrets:
       secretname: 
       secretdir:

4. Add a flag for each password entry to indicate the key in the secrets:

       superuser:
          username: #supername
          password: #superpassword

Questions:

* does this make sense?
* what token can we use to indicate a shared secret lookup key?

Notes:

* I will do the implementation of the above for Kubernetes.  Implementing it for other shared secret tools will be up to someone else.

issue_title: Patroni should enforce the replication user password
issue_body:
At the moment, someone may change the password for the replication user on the master. This would result in a mismatch between the password written by Patroni to the pgpass in order to initialize the replica and the one that is actually in the database, breaking creation of new replicas. There is no way we can prevent the superuser from changing the password, so we cannot guarantee that already running master will keep the password, but at least we can set the password on an already existing cluster started by Patroni. This way, if someone runs Patroni on an already existing cluster that has a standby user with the password different from the one in the YAML file, they will be able to have replicas running normally.

The actual use case for this is initialization of the new Spilo cluster from the EBS snapshot corresponding to the old cluster. Since the password for the Spilo is normally set to a random value, and will not match the one stored in the database in the snapshot, the master initialized from the snapshot will use the old password and won't be able to talk to the replicas spawned by the autoscaling group, that will use the password from the spilo/patroni configuration (i.e. the random one).

issue_title: pyral.context.Pinger considered harmful
issue_body:
Before attempting to connect to Rally via HTTP, pyral uses the pyral.context.Pinger class to attempt to ping the server to see if it is reachable. This is problematic for several reasons:

  - It is implemented by calling out to an external "ping" binary, which may or may not be available
    - Some security-conscious administrators are prone to removing the setuid bit from the ping binary, rendering it useless to anyone but root
  - Instead of using the tmpfile module, it writes the output of this call to a file called ".ping-bucket" in the current working directory, which is problematic for a number of reasons
    - This presumes that the current working directory is writable, and fails badly if it isn't
    - If you're running multiple instances of a script that use pyral in parallel, they could end up stomping on each other
    - Predictable temporary file names are a favorite attack vector of malicious individuals
  - It assumes that the host it is running on is able to transmit and receive ICMP ping packets
    - Some admittedly-misguided administrators are prone to filtering all ICMP
    - A host being pingable or not pingable is not a particularly good predictor of being reachable or not reachable via HTTP

I was going to submit a pull request to update Pinger to use tmpfile instead of "./.ping-bucket" but upon further consideration I think it would be better for it to be removed entirely. All it does is introduce several more ways to fail, which may or may not actually have anything to do with the availability of the Rally API. If there are network issues that are preventing you from reaching the API, then the HTTP connection will fail, and you can report the error from that, instead of trying some arbitrary test first.

issue_title: Comparison of issues and ordered set for ResultList rather than list
issue_body:
Hi guys,

Sorry for my english.

* I've played with the package and found that it behaves a little bit strange, for instance:

    ```python
    In [4]: issue = jira.issue('CDAS-782')
    In [5]: issue2 = jira.issue('CDAS-782')
    
    In [6]: issue == issue2  
    Out[6]: False
    
    In [7]: issues = jira.search_issues('key in (CDAS-782, CDAS-783)')
    In [8]: issues 
    Out[8]: [<JIRA Issue: key=u'CDAS-783', id=u'65593'>,
             <JIRA Issue: key=u'CDAS-782', id=u'65523'>]
    
    In [9]: issue in issues
    Out[9]: False
    ```
    
    That probably might be fixed by implementing of ``__eq__`` method for ``Issue`` class, or even for         ``Resource`` class if that makes sense. Something like the following should be enough:
    
    ```python
    def __eq__(self, other):
        return self.id == other.id
    ```

* The second thing I'd like to raise with you is using of an ordered set as base class for ``ResultList`` class as boards and issues are going to be unique across a project. If I understand you decided to use a class inherited from ``list`` as it's necessary to keep order of issues returned by server 'cause it's important if we make JQL request that ends with `ORDER BY any_field [ASC|DESC]`.
But lists are not supposed to have unique objects so if we need to keep order and keep uniqueness we should use ordered set instead. In addition it would be great to have some set's benefits (I mean algebra of sets).

It was just a superficial glance and I would appreciate if you share your thoughts.

Thanks,
Roman

issue_title: search_issues (Get all) is broken.
issue_body:
##Summary
search_issues (Returns only the batch size, which is 50,  when the caller expects to the total number of issues back.)
  |-> _fetch_pages 
        |-> _get_json (response does not contain 'isLast')

##Recommendation
The _fetch_pages seems at quick glance to have multiple intentions that might be more suitable separated into different functions. 

The VAR `is_last` is set once to True and therfore always breaks the while condition "for the case of basic JIRA API".

##Solution 
For this issue a fix most suitable of the current code would be to add an additional variable that translates the client parameter maxResults from a falsy value to a "get_all" truthy value. The truthy value could then branch instruction flow as follows.

`if get_all:
    while page_start < total and len(next_items_page) <= page_size:
    ....
else:
    while not is_last and (total is None or page_start < total) and len(next_items_page) == page_size:`
    ....
`

##Explanation
The _fetch_pages relies upon _get_json functionality. 
`resource = self._get_json(request_path, params=page_params, base=base)`
           
The returned dictionary is expected to have 'isLast' key with a boolean value, but does not, therefore defaults to true.
`if isinstance(resource, dict):
                total = resource.get('total')
                # 'isLast' is the optional key added to responses in JIRA Agile 6.7.6. So far not used in basic JIRA API.
                is_last = resource.get('isLast', True)
                start_at_from_response = resource.get('startAt', 0)
                max_results_from_response = resource.get('maxResults', 1)
            else:
                # if is a list
                total = 1
                is_last = True
                start_at_from_response = 0
                max_results_from_response = 1
`
causing the following conditional statement to be False.
`
if not maxResults:
                page_size = max_results_from_response or len(items)
                page_start = (startAt or start_at_from_response or 0) + page_size
                while not is_last and (total is None or page_start < total) and len(next_items_page) == page_size:
`

issue_title: "Uncaught ReferenceError: Cookielaw is not defined" when javascript is wrapped in an anonimous function
issue_body:
Using [django-pipeline](https://github.com/jazzband/django-pipeline/blob/master/docs/using.rst) for static compression, all javascript output is wrapped in [an anonymous function](http://django-pipeline.readthedocs.io/en/latest/configuration.html), as security measure.
>This safety wrapper, make it difficult to pollute the global namespace by accident and improve performance.

The click on button *I agree* fire `Cookielaw.createCookielawCookie();` and generate the exception `Uncaught ReferenceError: Cookielaw is not defined(…)`

issue_title: Upload is slower for very large because of md5 calculation
issue_body:
For larger file uploads, takes too long mainly because of file_hash (md5) calculation.

This is line is the line responsible for this 
    mpu = bucket.initiate_multipart_upload(s3url.path, metadata = {'md5': self.file_hash(source), 'privilege': self.get_file_privilege(source)})

https://github.com/bloomreach/s4cmd/blob/master/s4cmd.py#L1043

I've read in aws documentation that this md5 metadata is not necessary, I suspect this md5 calculation exists mainly because local md5 of file is different from the generated by S3.

It is used in `sync_check`:
  ('md5' in remoteKey.metadata and remoteKey.metadata['md5'] == localmd5)

It is useful mostly for calls that use sync option.

But sometimes, like in my case, the user of s4cmd may not care about sync, since he may using the -f (force) option.

I thought about two possible solutions:

- One argument to just disable this file_hash execution like "--disable-multipart-meta-md5"

- The second one is more ambitious, and I don't know if it is really possible:

Don't calculate the md5, but uses s3's generated md5 for calculation. (We will need to reproduce s3's md5 calculation)

S3 calculates the s3 etag for multipart uploads using a md5 of the md5 of splitted parts. The algorithm is described in these links:

http://stackoverflow.com/questions/6591047/etag-definition-changed-in-amazon-s3
http://permalink.gmane.org/gmane.comp.file-systems.s3.s3tools/583
http://stackoverflow.com/questions/12186993/what-is-the-algorithm-to-compute-the-amazon-s3-etag-for-a-file-larger-than-5gb
https://forums.aws.amazon.com/thread.jspa?messageID=203510

The thing that is missing from to recalculate s3's md5 for multipart is the SPLIT_SIZE.

But s3's e-tag for file has a sufix -NUMBER_OF_PARTS. 

    s3cmd --recursive --list-md5 ls s3://YOURBUCKET/YOURDIR/ 
    2015-02-13 05:02 9791602688   f938b15b2edef7d2c23542814bdcb6af-187  s3://FILEPATH

I guess with this info size (and number of parts), we could use to calculate like amazon's s3 does

In the example above:

    parts = 187
    file_size = toMB(9791602688.0D) # 9338.0
    
    SPLIT_SIZE = ((file_size - (file_size % parts)) / parts) + 1
    SPLIT_SIZE = 50 # MB

issue_title: Using CORSConfig and setting max_age affects all requests, not just OPTIONS
issue_body:
Reproduction steps:

Create a custom cors config and set the max_age:
`customCors = CORSConfig(
    allow_origin="*",
    max_age=86400
)`

Now use that in a route:
`@app.route('/users/{userId}', methods=['GET'], cors=customCors)`

As expected, the OPTIONS calls get cached because the response header sets `Access-Control-Max-Age` properly.  However, the GET call to /users/{userId} also includes Access-Control-Max-Age in the response headers, which is not expected behavior.  Shouldn't that only be set on the OPTIONS response?

issue_title: Solution for large ids by adding with O(1) lookup
issue_body:
In your documentation it reads:

> Using Redis bitmaps you can store events for millions of users in a very little amount of memory (megabytes). You should be careful about using huge ids (e.g. 2^32 or bigger) as this could require larger amounts of memory.

It might be a potential solution to create a hash table that keeps track of huge ids and maps them back down to smaller indexes.

For example, a user with an id of 192329230202 could be mapped to a smaller index 1 in the bitmap. This would require an O(1) lookup before a `SETBIT' so it shouldn't affect time performance, but it would require more space on disk. 

Steps to implement:

1. A new user performs an signup event. 
1. Find the users_counter for the current day `GET "users_counter:20160614"` which would respond back with something like 2. 
1. Add new `User(id:192329230202)` to the `user_index` table and reassign to `User(internal_id:3)`. This would do something like `SET "users_index:20160614:192329230202" 3`
1. Once this is completed successfully you would want to run `INCR users_count:20160614`
1. `SETBIT "events:search:20160614" 3 1` bit into feature signup at `User(3)` index instead of at the end of the bitmap which requires creating and storing empty bits. 

This would most likely add complexity into how your query the data and you would have to store a reference to lookup each user. In my proposal I used a different lookup per day to reset the bitmap indexes everyday, but this might be more trouble than just maintaining one large ongoing table.

Let me know your thoughts and if this something you are interested in promoting into an enhancement.

issue_title: unittestt.SkipTest is treated as failure in setUpClass() by testtools.run
issue_body:
When running a test suite built with stdlib unittest (running with Python 3.6.5) if a setUpClass() method raises a unittest.SkipTest exception to skip the test the testtools.run runner is treating that as an error. For example using a synthetic example like:

```
import unittest

class TestDirectSkip(unittest.TestCase):

    @classmethod
    def setUpClass(cls):
        super(TestDirectSkip, cls).setUpClass()
        cls.test = 'a'
        raise unittest.SkipTest("I really skip")

    def test_foo(self):
        self.assertEqual(self.test, 'a')
```
will return a failure like:

```
Tests running...
======================================================================
ERROR: setUpClass (test_skips.TestDirectSkip)
----------------------------------------------------------------------
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/tmp/tests/test_skips.py", line 29, in setUpClass
    raise unittest.case.SkipTest("I really skip")
unittest.case.SkipTest: I really skip
```

My thinking it it has something to do with: https://github.com/testing-cabal/testtools/blob/master/testtools/testcase.py#L66-L69 because if you switch the exception from unittest to unittest2 in the example like:

```
import unittest

class TestDirectSkip(unittest.TestCase):

    @classmethod
    def setUpClass(cls):
        super(TestDirectSkip, cls).setUpClass()
        cls.test = 'a'
        import unittest2
        raise unittest2.SkipTest("I really skip")

    def test_foo(self):
        self.assertEqual(self.test, 'a')
```
The skip is properly recognized.

issue_title: Logging Handlers add the attribute python_logger which breaks the stackdriver convention for jsonpayload
issue_body:
# Thank you for reporting an issue to google-cloud-python!

If you are reporting an issue or requesting a feature, please search the
existing open and closed issues to see if there is already work being done.

- https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/google-cloud-python/issues
- http://stackoverflow.com/questions/tagged/google-cloud-python

If you can provide us with as much of the following information as possible it
will help us identify the cause of your issue more quickly.

1. Specify the API at the beginning of the title (for example, "BigQuery: ...")
   General, Core, and Other are also allowed as types
ClougLogging
2. OS type and version
AppEngine Flex Python 3
3. Python version and virtual environment information `python --version`
AppEngine Python3
4. google-cloud-python version `pip show google-cloud`, `pip show google-<service>` or `pip freeze`
Name: google-cloud-logging
Version: 1.6.0
Summary: Stackdriver Logging API client library
Home-page: https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/google-cloud-python
Author: Google LLC
Author-email: googleapis-packages@google.com
License: Apache 2.0
Location: /Users/joseret/g/pso/demo/jrdemo-auth/buildapp/client1venv/lib/python3.7/site-packages
Requires: google-cloud-core, google-api-core
Required-by:

5. Stacktrace if available
n/a
6. Steps to reproduce
logging.warning('{ "time": "2018-08-13 16:23:44.497Z",  "severity", "WARNING",  "message": ' + json.dumps({'a': 'a1', 'b': 'a2'}))
cloud_logger.warning('{ "time": "2018-08-13 16:23:44.497Z",  "severity", "WARNING",  "message": ' + json.dumps({'a': 'a1', 'b': 'a2'}))

7. Code example

logging.warning('{ "time": "2018-08-13 16:23:44.497Z",  "severity", "WARNING",  "message": ' + json.dumps({'a': 'a1', 'b': 'a2'}))
cloud_logger.warning('{ "time": "2018-08-13 16:23:44.497Z",  "severity", "WARNING",  "message": ' + json.dumps({'a': 'a1', 'b': 'a2'}))

results in 
   jsonPayload: {
  message: "{ "time": "2018-08-13 16:23:44.497Z",  "msg": "text", "c": "c1", "d": "d1" }"   
  python_logger: "root"   
 }

This is the issue:

JSON detection in the agent will only kick in if, besides "time" and "severity", the payload contains exactly one field (named one of "log", "message", or "msg"). Your payloads all contain an extra field called "python_logger", which disables JSON detection. We should be better at making this explicit — it's mentioned in the description of the "message" field at https://cloud.google.com/logging/docs/agent/configuration#special_fields_in_structured_payloads.
        Igor
Using GitHub flavored markdown can help make your request clearer.
See: https://guides.github.com/features/mastering-markdown/

issue_title: `protobuf-3.0.0` is missing `_ _init_ _.py`, and is not recognized by Python v2.7.6
issue_body:
From Python 2.7.6., the package results in the following error:

```
ImportError: No module named google.protobuf
```

In order to get the package to become discoverable, I had to add an empty `__init__.py` file to the `site-packages/google` directory.

```
touch /usr/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/google/__init__.py
```

Here is the [stackoverflow post](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/38680593/importerror-no-module-named-google-protobuf) that led me to the solution, and it is also the post that directs everyone to use the `google` package that is incorrectly attributed to you (#4345).

issue_title: Avoid using dependency version ranges
issue_body:
Due to the issue with google-http-java-client https://github.com/google/google-http-java-client/issues/438 our production build just started to fail today morning. Such an issue shouldn't threaten anyone's build, but such errors can escalate quickly is dependency ranges are used in released dependencies.
The version `<version>[1.19,2.0)</version>` used in the pom should be replaced to a fix, release version to ensure build stability.

yarn install

issue_title: Cannot create domain record with V2 API
issue_body:
https://github.com/devo-ps/dopy/blob/master/dopy/manager.py#L362

In V2, DO API return a "domain_record" json object, not "record" as in V1, see: https://developers.digitalocean.com/documentation/v2/#domain-records

Other domain record functions are ok.

issue_title: Exception ImproperlyConfigured trying to instantiate a DataTableView embedded in an unrelated template
issue_body:
Hi All,
I’m trying to create a TemplateView which has a bunch of simple widgets followed by 2 embedded DataTableViews, following the sample ‘SatelliteDataTableView’.  I keep getting the above exception after trying several things, so would appreciate some suggestions about how to do embedded views.

But in this code:
```python
## this is my desired ‘embedded’ Datatable
class FileDatatable( Datatable):
    class Meta:
        columns = ['directory', 'name' , 'size' , 'creDate' , 'modDate']
        structure_template = "dtUi/bootstrap_structure.html"

## this is my desired ‘satellite’ view
class FileDatatableView( DatatableView):
    model = File
    datatable_class = FileDatatable
    template_name = 'dtui/file_list.html'

	## added this method as my second try, after peeking at the actual sample code (as opposed to the documented implementation), but no help.
    def get_datatable_kwargs(self):
        """added to the vanilla view to make the view embeddable via instantiation.  See py:class:`FormWithTablesView` below."""
        kwargs = super(FileDatatableView, self).get_datatable_kwargs()
        kwargs['url'] = reverse('dtUi:first_try')       ## a bit ugly that the view can only be attached to one URL
        kwargs['template_name'] = 'dtui/file_list.html'
        return kwargs
	## added this method as my third try, believing the text of the error message.  Also no help.
    def get_template_names(self):
        """
        Also added to support embeddable view.  Still don't know why, though.
        """
        return ['dtui/file_list.html']

## this is my desired master view with 2 Datatables (removed the other widgets in the interest of simplicity)
class FormWithTablesView( TemplateView):
    template = 'dtUi/form_with_tables.html'

    def get_context_data(self, **kwargs):
        context = super(FormWithTablesView, self).get_context_data(**kwargs)

        context['ftTable'] = FileDatatableView().get_datatable()
	 ## the first error I actually got pertained to missing URL definition.  I was able to get past that with the following:
        ##context['ftTable'] = FileDatatableView().get_datatable(url=reverse(‘dtui/file_list.html’))
	 ## but then I encountered the persistent template_name issue…

        context['ftelTable'] = FileDatatableView_EditLink().get_datatable()	## assume that FileDatatableView_EditLink is a copy of FileDatatableView with different columns.
        return context
```

I’m blocked by this exception: 

```
Internal Server Error: /ui/fwt/
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "N:\vePython\django\lib\site-packages\django\core\handlers\base.py", line 132, in get_response
    response = wrapped_callback(request, *callback_args, **callback_kwargs)
  File "N:\vePython\django\lib\site-packages\django\views\generic\base.py", line 71, in view
    return self.dispatch(request, *args, **kwargs)
  File "N:\vePython\django\lib\site-packages\django\views\generic\base.py", line 89, in dispatch
    return handler(request, *args, **kwargs)
  File "N:\vePython\django\lib\site-packages\django\views\generic\base.py", line 159, in get
    return self.render_to_response(context)
  File "N:\vePython\django\lib\site-packages\django\views\generic\base.py", line 133, in render_to_response
    template=self.get_template_names(),
  File "N:\vePython\django\lib\site-packages\django\views\generic\base.py", line 146, in get_template_names
    "TemplateResponseMixin requires either a definition of "
django.core.exceptions.ImproperlyConfigured: TemplateResponseMixin requires either a definition of 'template_name' or an implementation of 'get_template_names()'
[16/Nov/2015 21:09:07] "GET /ui/fwt/ HTTP/1.1" 500 98707
```
I’m running these versions of things:

Python 3.5.0 (v3.5.0:374f501f4567, Sep 13 2015, 02:27:37) [MSC v.1900 64 bit (AMD64)] on win32
Django (1.8.6)
django-datatable-view (0.9.0b5, n:\devwork\django\django-datatable-view)

And, as you can see, I’m pretty far off in the weeds and have no clue how to proceed.
Any suggestions you offer will be gratefully accepted.

Regards,
Bob

issue_title: SIGQUIT is a bad choice for graceful restart
issue_body:
Current code uses SIGQUIT for graceful restart, however that code never gets executed because SIGQUIT means unclean shutdown. Some platforms will not allow app to handle this signal.

Proper graceful shutdown signal should be one of SIGINT, SIGHUP, SIGUSR1 or SIGUSR2.

Please refer to https://mail.python.org/pipermail/python-ideas/2016-February/038471.html

issue_title: Unable to set StringValue on ObjectField in DataPipeline
issue_body:
I'm unable to properly set a StringValue inside of an ObjectField resource when creating a DataPipeline. 

This error gets returned from CloudFormation:
```
Value of property StringValue must be of type String
```

Snippit of troposphere code:
```
        schedule = PipelineObject(
            Fields=[ObjectField(
                Key='type',
                StringValue=['Schedule']
            ), ObjectField(
                Key='occurences',
                StringValue=['2']
            ), ObjectField(
                Key='startAt',
                StringValue=['FIRST_ACTIVATION_DATE_TIME']
            ), ObjectField(
                Key='period',
                StringValue=['1 day']
            )],
            Id= 'S3_Schedule',
            Name='S3Schedule'
        )
```

I believe this is due to @philtay's latest commit (https://github.com/cloudtools/troposphere/commit/a1957fb04118ce13f2cb37a52bc93718eed0ae41), which now expects StringValue to be an array. 

If I specify the StringValue as a string and not an array, troposphere fails to build the cloudformation as well:
```
TypeError: StringValue is <type 'str'>, expected [<type 'basestring'>, <class 'troposphere.Ref'>]
```

I could, however, be declaring the ObjectField's completely wrong.
