issue_title: cache keys not matching cross-platform with v2 engine
issue_body:
I'm running pants 1.3.0rc2.  As far as I know, this was an issue on rc1 and rc0 as well.  We have two continuous integration machines (Centos 7.3.1611) that run the build with every commit.  Our developer machines are running osx.  I started noticing that the developer machines were not finding any hits from the cache created on the build machines.  All zinc entries would miss.  Also, entries for one of our custom tasks would miss.  The debug logs showed that the requests to the cache were for keys that didn't exist in the cache and 404s were returned.

The cache did work between the two build machines.  One of the build machines would find hits for artifacts created on the other machine and vice-versa.  It was only the developer machines missing.

I first did a comparison of options between the build options on the build machines and developer machine.  I found no significant differences, only things like python path.  I also used the same pants.ini file on the developer machine and still got misses.

I wrote a task to dump out `target.invalidation_hash(None)` on a small target and ran this on both machines.  This hash differs.  If I dump all fields of the payload of the target, I see that `field.sources.files_hash` differs for the `sources` field of the target.

I used python to compute the sha1 of the files and their paths.  These all match, but the `invalidation_hash` is still different.  On both machines the manually computed hash is different from `invalidation_hash`.

When I switch to the old engine, invalidation_hash matches on the machines, and also matches my manually computed version of the hash.  The cache works normally between machines (mostly hits) when I switch to the old engine.

I will attach the source of my task that dumps info about the targets and the output of this on both machines.

issue_title: test --enable-v2-engine (as a global default) in master
issue_body:
in order to discover unknown bugs in areas of pants that are untested by our internal CI sandboxing, we'll also want to run all of the pants test with this flag enabled.

there are a few approaches to this:

1) expand the usage of the `@ensure_engine` decorator (whitelist approach)
2) enable the behavior of `@ensure_engine` by default and invert the whitelisting approach in #1 to except specific tests (blacklist approach)
3) enable a completely separate jenkins job/configuration to mirror CI with `--enable-v2-engine` as default configuration

additionally, initial smoke testing via PR may prove useful in uncovering an initial survey of the state of things - as well as informing the initial round of new bugs shaken out from this.

On a machine with a lot of CPU usage, hitting this timeout is pretty easy:
https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/blob/ab40cc17389859ac0ab6879e826c766c5d5f8eb3/src/python/pants/pantsd/pants_daemon.py#L322-L323

issue_body:
I am experimenting with using `deploy_excludes` in junit-runner to fix a problem I'm having with running a particular kind of test suite.  I noticed that building a jar file with 0.0.53 and deploy_excludes=[] appears to be broken, or I don't understand how it is supposed to work.

Here's the patch I was working with:

```
diff --git a/src/java/org/pantsbuild/tools/junit/BUILD b/src/java/org/pantsbuild/tools/junit/BUILD
index 3738214..f72d1cf 100644
--- a/src/java/org/pantsbuild/tools/junit/BUILD
+++ b/src/java/org/pantsbuild/tools/junit/BUILD
@@ -31,6 +31,9 @@ jvm_binary(
   dependencies=[
     ':junit',
   ],
+  deploy_excludes=[
+    exclude(name='junit', org='junit'),
+  ],
   description="""
 A replacement for org.junit.runner.JUnitCore.main that adds:
   + support for running individual test methods using [classname]#[methodname]
```

I created jars with 0.0.51 and 0.0.52 respectively and renamed the output

```
$ ls -l junit*
-rw-r--r--  1 zundel  zundel  2701454 Oct 11 07:03 junit-runner-0.0.51.jar
-rw-r--r--  1 zundel  zundel  3068891 Oct 11 07:17 junit-runner-0.0.52.jar
```

The difference is that the version created with 0.0.51 doesn't contain the org.junit classes, but the one created with 0.0.52 still contains them (as if deploy_excludes was not specified.)

```
$ jar -tvf junit-runner-0.0.51.jar | grep "org/junit" | wc -l
       0
$ jar -tvf junit-runner-0.0.52.jar | grep "org/junit" | wc -l
     284
```

  S3Listener:

      Handler: com.example.MyListener
      Runtime: java8
      CodeUri: target/example.jar

I assume that my Lambda is called if a file ending with '.jpg' is uploaded to MyBucket. From my understanding, this should be supported by SAM according to the documentation linked above. I hope you can help me!

issue_title: Fix Documentation: Document the default LogicalIds created by SAM
issue_body:
SAM users were unable to figure out what ${ServerlessRestApi} referred to in this example:
https://github.com/awslabs/serverless-application-model/blob/master/examples/2016-10-31/encryption_proxy/template.yaml

'ServerlessRestApi’ is the logicalId for the implicit API that gets created if you have the event type as API in your AWS::Serverless::Function, but it is not pointing to any API explicitly defined in the template. 

This is not documented anywhere.

issue_title: template resource
issue_body:
One of the downsides of the micro services architecture model is that there is more overhead because the configuration, management and deployment tasks are duplicated for each service.  Our swagger schemas and Cloudformation templates are full of copy-and-pasted snippets.

In the example below, the commonality is extracted into `LambdaTemplate`, reducing the `PutFunction` to 3 lines and allowing the differences between the other functions to be more obvious.

```yaml
AWSTemplateFormatVersion: '2010-09-09'
Transform: AWS::Serverless-2016-10-31
Resources:
  LambdaTemplate:
    Type: AWS::Serverless::TemplateFunction
    Properties:
      Handler: index.handler
      Runtime: nodejs4.3
      Policies: AWSLambdaDynamoDBExecutionRole
      CodeUri: s3://bucketName/codepackage.zip
      Environment:
        Variables:
          TABLE_NAME: !Ref Table

  PutFunction:
    Type: AWS::Serverless::Function
    Properties:
      # extra points if this can (optionally) be imported from an external file, even in a separate but shared repository
      Template: !Ref LambdaTemplate
  GetFunction:
    Type: AWS::Serverless::Function
    Properties:
      Template: !Ref LambdaTemplate
      Handler: index.getHandler
  DeleteFunction:
    Type: AWS::Serverless::Function
    Properties:
      Template: !Ref LambdaTemplate
      Events:
        Stream:
          Type: DynamoDB
          Properties:
            Stream: !GetAtt DynamoDBTable.StreamArn
            BatchSize: 100
            StartingPosition: TRIM_HORIZON

   DynamoDBTable:
     Type: AWS::DynamoDB::Table
     Properties: 
       AttributeDefinitions: 
         - AttributeName: id
           AttributeType: S
       KeySchema: 
         - AttributeName: id
           KeyType: HASH
       ProvisionedThroughput: 
         ReadCapacityUnits: 5
         WriteCapacityUnits: 5
       StreamSpecification:
         StreamViewType: streamview type
```

issue_title: Different bait credentials for different IPs
issue_body:
It would be nice to have possibility to use certain baits only in drones in certain network segments (like 123.123.0.0) or only in certain specific IP (like 123.123.123.123).

In Bait Credentials view this would be just extra field for each bait.

By default they would be used in every drone (for example 0.0.0.0).

I am not sure if the server should select drones where to send baits with other configurations or if the server would send them to all drones - who would then check themselves what baits to use.

issue_title: Cannot parse the correct scheme of URL in Python26
issue_body:
I work on CentOS 6.6 and Python is 2.6.6. Pushgateway runs on localhost:9091.

When I push metrics with a URL starts with neither "http://" or "https://", just like `push_to_gateway('localhost:9091', job='demo', registry=registry)`, error occurs as follow:
```
# python push_demo.py 
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "push_demo.py", line 19, in <module>
    push_to_gateway('localhost:9091', job='dev', registry=registry)
  File "/usr/lib/python2.6/site-packages/prometheus_client-0.0.21-py2.6.egg/prometheus_client/exposition.py", line 198, in push_to_gateway
    _use_gateway('PUT', gateway, job, registry, grouping_key, timeout, handler)
  File "/usr/lib/python2.6/site-packages/prometheus_client-0.0.21-py2.6.egg/prometheus_client/exposition.py", line 265, in _use_gateway
    headers=headers, data=data)()
  File "/usr/lib/python2.6/site-packages/prometheus_client-0.0.21-py2.6.egg/prometheus_client/exposition.py", line 133, in handle
    resp = build_opener(HTTPHandler).open(request, timeout=timeout)
  File "/usr/lib64/python2.6/urllib2.py", line 391, in open
    response = self._open(req, data)
  File "/usr/lib64/python2.6/urllib2.py", line 414, in _open
    'unknown_open', req)
  File "/usr/lib64/python2.6/urllib2.py", line 369, in _call_chain
    result = func(*args)
  File "/usr/lib64/python2.6/urllib2.py", line 1223, in unknown_open
    raise URLError('unknown url type: %s' % type)
urllib2.URLError: <urlopen error unknown url type: localhost>
```
I think it's a bug of `urlparse` in Python26.
```
Python 2.6.6 (r266:84292, Jan 22 2014, 09:42:36) 
[GCC 4.4.7 20120313 (Red Hat 4.4.7-4)] on linux2
Type "help", "copyright", "credits" or "license" for more information.
>>> import urlparse
>>> urlparse.urlparse('localhost:9091')
ParseResult(scheme='localhost', netloc='', path='9091', params='', query='', fragment='')
>>>
```

issue_title: Discovery not reporting any speaker on Windows
issue_body:
Hi,
I just tried soco 0.15 on Python 3.7.0 on Windows 10, and discovery() doesn't yield anything.
`>>> import soco
>>> soco.discover()
>>> s = soco.SoCo("xxx.xxx.xxx.xxx")
>>> s
SoCo("xxx.xxx.xxx.xxx")
>>> s.get_speaker_info()
{'player_icon': '/img/icon-ZP90.png', 'mac_address': ... }
`
I tried also with the interface_addr to no avail. Firewall lets all necessary multicast go through (the sonos controller sees the devices). The network is configured as a /16, and the computer is on the same layer2 segment as the SoCos connect devices.
On Linux, soco 0.15, Python 3.5.2 it works fine. I don't have a lot more platforms to test on, but might if it's interesting (I guess Windows isn't the default choice for most of you ;))

issue_title: Disable submit buttons when busy
issue_body:
### Observed behavior

In most places, we do not disable save/submit buttons, which can result in multi-submission. Ideally we would disable these buttons. This is very common in modals but also exists elsewhere.

### Expected behavior

Notify the user that the request is in progress + prevent accidental multi submission. Use a loader?

### User-facing consequences

Unsure if the request was successful.

issue_body:
Per this design, there is a 'last updated' column, which indicates the last time the channel was updated or downloaded for the first time.

https://projects.invisionapp.com/share/KDB49AZTW#/screens/226789607

This feature requires a place to store this data that can be cross-referenced with the channelId.

issue_title: PDF zoom gestures broken on mobile
issue_body:
### Observed behavior

When trying to zoom in and out of a PDF on mobile using gestures, unexpected behaviors happen. For example, it's possible to zoom in the whole window such that the text becomes pixelated.

This behavior happens in both full-screen and embedded modes, but is more disconcerting in full-screen mode.

### Expected behavior

Zoom gestures should either be disabled or work as expected

### User-facing consequences

User may zoom using gestures, which makes the text larger but is still hard to read due to pixelation.

We should either disable or properly handle zoom gestures

### Steps to reproduce

use pinch-zoom gestures in the PDF renderer 

### Context

K09, chrome on iphone

issue_title: On removing the previously recorded answer from a question in exam, It still stores the previous data.
issue_body:
## Summary
On removing the previously recorded answer from a question in exam, It still stores the previous answer.

## System information
 - Version: Kolibri 0.4.0aplha7
 - Operating system: Ubuntu 14.04 LTS
 - Browser: Chrome

## How to reproduce

1. Attempted a question in exam.
2. Click on next question.
2. Go back to the question and clear the option.

**Expected result**
The cleared/deleted answer should go away from the question.

**Actual Result**
The answer entered is saved and does not delete, and the Question indicator also shows attempted only.

issue_title: add multi-language stop-word de-prioritization to search
issue_body:
### Observed behavior

searches such as "what is an LED" can produce unhelpful results because of the commonality of some words in the phrase

### Expected behavior

common [stop words](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Stop_words) should be filtered out of our searches. see for example https://github.com/6/stopwords-json

### User-facing consequences

see some [results of testing search queries](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1zIGjlNyiXbJW4cABtakkqZkdjejGV-KRIc5XgwiDy2w/edit)



### Context

0.9

issue_title: misp_get_event(eventid) doesn't return tags for event
issue_body:
I'm using PyMISP to to do some processing on events in MISP

As part of the data I'm processing I would like to have the tags for the event(s), but the json returned from misp_get_event() doesn't have the tags.

I'm using the following code:
```
misp = PyMISP("https://%s/" % misp_server, misp_key, False, 'json')
source_event = misp.get_event(source_id)
```

I see that get_stix_event() have an tags parameter to include tags, but since I use json for everything else I would like to avoid rewriting the code to use STIX instead of json

issue_title: bytes rearranged in rf_data for rx_explicit
issue_body:
First of all, thank you for a great library, it is very easy to use!

I am trying to communicate with ZigBee devices via XBee XStick2 ZB. Communication works perfectly, but the data I receive in `rf_data` field makes no sense (according to ZDO). After additional investigation I figured out that the bytes might be there, but they seem to be rearranged - and not in little endian / big endian kind of way (as far as I can tell).

The easiest example to see what is going on is when I send a request with cluster 0x0005. I get response with cluster 0x8005 as expected:

    RESPONSE: {'profile': '\x00\x00', 'source_addr': '\xD2\xDD', 'dest_endpoint': '\x00', 'rf_data': '\xDD\x80\xD2\x05\x00', 'source_endpoint': '\x00', 'options': '\x41', 'source_addr_long': '\x00\x50\x43\xC9\xA3\x36\x8B\x61', 'cluster': '\x80\x05', 'id': 'rx_explicit'}

All other fields look OK, but `rf_data` has this value:

    \xDD\x80\xD2\x05\x00

ZDO documentation says that the fields should be:

-----------
**Active Endpoints Response**
*Cluster ID: 0x8005*
Description: Indicates the list of active endpoints supported on the device.

Field Name | Size (bytes) | Description
---------- | ------------ | ----------- 
Status | 1 | 
Network Address | 2 | Indicates the 16-bit address of the responding device
Active Endpoint Count | 1 | Number of endpoints in the following endpoint list
Active Endpoint List | Variable | List of endpoints supported on the destination device. One byte per
endpoint.
------------

Network Address in this case should be the same as in field `source_addr`, that is: `\xD2\xDD`. Note that I have tried this command with different devices and I always get the bytes from `source_addr` in the same places of `rf_data` (0 and 2). For instance:

source_addr | rf_data
----------- | -------
'\x00\x00' | '\x**00**\x81\x**00**\x7E'
'\xD2\xDD' | '\x**DD**\x80\x**D2**\x05\x00'
'\x6E\xF7' | '\x**F7**\x80\x**6E**\x05\x00'
'\x9C\x81' | '\x**81**\x80\x**9C**\x05\x00'
... | ...

So, `source_addr` is there but on wrong address, and I am missing value `\x01` (Active Endpoint Count). My guess is that there is either a problem with my USB stick or with parsing `rf_data` field (more likely?), but I was unable to find the reason (would need to know rx_explicit format to see what is going on). 

I have also checked the code from http://www.desert-home.com/2014/10/ok-back-to-zigbee-protocol-and-xbees.html but unfortunately I don't have a ZigBee switch at hand. It seems that author (and commenters) had some problems with inexplicable bytes in rf_data too, though not as much as myself.

I would appreciate some pointers...

    >>> xbee.__version__
    '2.2.5'

(installed via `pip install xbee`)

issue_title: 'charm login' requests my password, promises to be good, violates oauth
issue_body:
Rather than redirect me to the SSO website where I can enter my password securely, 'charm login' instead requests I enter my password and posts it on my behalf to the SSO website. This requires me to trust charm-tools with my password, and the libraries it uses to correctly verify the chain of trust and refuse to continue if the SSL certificate is not valid (MITM attacks), and does not give me an opportunity to confirm the access level being requested. These are all reasons that the oauth protocol exists in the first place ("OAuth is an authentication protocol that allows users to approve application to act on their behalf without sharing their password.")

What should happen is it opens a browser window to the SSO website where I can login if necessary, do the 2fa dance if necessary, and confirm that I wish to grant the requested access.

issue_title: Fetching KYC documents on unsaved account results in an internal server error
issue_body:
A small bug in our app led it to do something similar to `NaturalUser().documents.all()`. If you try that you'll see that it sends an API request for `/users/None/KYC/documents`, which is obviously wrong and results in a 500 response (a 404 or 400 would be more appropriate, but that's a separate issue).

It would be better to avoid making API requests that are doomed to fail, instead the python code should either raise an appropriate exception or log a warning and return an empty list.

issue_title: Fix NIC.dhcp bool-to-str convertion
issue_body:
In the code of `create_nic()`, you have:

```
properties['dhcp'] = str(loadbalancer.dhcp).lower()
```

If the NIC was created with dhcp set to any explession that returns an object, but not True/False, this would lead to sending a string representation of the object to the API. 

Easy fix — convert it to a boolean first:

```
properties['dhcp'] = str(bool(loadbalancer.dhcp)).lower()
```

The same is for `LoadBalancer.dhcp`.

The same is for `NIC.__repr__` and `LoadBalancer.__repr__`.

tbl = 'SCRAP'

tempDf = s.sd2df(table=tbl, libref=libref, dsopts={'where': where})

issue_title: Make check for best model (and early stopping) use >, not >=
issue_labels: [{'id': 887719346, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/allenai/allennlp/labels/Contributions%20welcome', 'name': 'Contributions welcome', 'color': 'e827b7', 'default': False}, {'id': 723800354, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/allenai/allennlp/labels/Good%20First%20Issue', 'name': 'Good First Issue', 'color': 'e99695', 'default': False}, {'id': 723800272, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/allenai/allennlp/labels/Help%20Wanted', 'name': 'Help Wanted', 'color': '7ee577', 'default': False}]
issue_body:
The way we're using `max` and `min` effectively does a `>=` check for whether current epoch is the best epoch, and we have that check in two different places (one for saving model weights, and one for early stopping).  We should use `>` instead of `>=`, and we should have only one check.

Benefits:
1. If two epochs get the same value for the validation metric, the earlier one is less likely to be overfit, and this change will make us keep the earlier model instead of the later model.
2. If you max out your validation metric, or just stop improving, you currently won't hit our early stopping criterion, because of the `>=`.  This will make you actually stop early.
3. Using one check instead of two simplifies the code.

issue_title: No defaults added to existing models changed to Versionable
issue_body:
When converting an existing `models.Model` model class to a `Versionable` model class, defaults are not provided, therefore the user must make decisions as part of `makemigration` migration step.  For the 2 examples below, Versionable should be able to handle these sensibly. For `identity` fields, it should copy the table's PK field, as this is what it does for new rows in the table. For date fields (example 2) it should be the database `"now()"` at the time it is run on the database, not a hard-coded value (like `timezone.now()`) in the migration.

**Example 1: Handle new `identity` column in existing table** 
```sh
You are trying to add a non-nullable field 'identity' to assessment without a default; we can't do that (the database needs something to populate existing rows).
Please select a fix:
 1) Provide a one-off default now (will be set on all existing rows)
 2) Quit, and let me add a default in models.py
Select an option: 1
Please enter the default value now, as valid Python
The datetime and django.utils.timezone modules are available, so you can do e.g. timezone.now()
>>> ''
```
**Example 1 Migration File Output**
```python
migrations.AddField(
    model_name='assessment',
    name='identity',
    field=models.CharField(default='', max_length=36),
    preserve_default=False,
),
```

**Example 2: Handle new `version date` column in existing table** 
```sh
You are trying to add a non-nullable field 'version_birth_date' to assessment without a default; we can't do that (the database needs something to populate existing rows).
Please select a fix:
 1) Provide a one-off default now (will be set on all existing rows)
 2) Quit, and let me add a default in models.py
Select an option: 1
Please enter the default value now, as valid Python
The datetime and django.utils.timezone modules are available, so you can do e.g. timezone.now()
>>> timezone.now()
```
**Example 2 Migration File Output**
```python
migrations.AddField(
    model_name='assessment',
    name='version_birth_date',
    field=models.DateTimeField(default=datetime.datetime(2015, 7, 20, 15, 30, 4, 971732, tzinfo=utc)),
    preserve_default=False,
),
```

issue_title: Add synchronous flavour of the API
issue_body:
The lib is currently based on callbacks which works well in the GUI based client. Callbacks can easily become messy though and adding a synchronous flavour of the API as well would simplify scripting of non GUI based applications.

An example:
1. Open a connection to a CF
2. Wait for a log value to reach a specific value
3. Set a parameter
4. Close the connection

```python
log_config = LogConfig(name='My data', period_in_ms=100)
log_config.add_variable('my.var', 'float')

with SyncCrazyflie('radio://0/60/2M') as cf:
    with SyncLogger(cf, log_config) as logger:
        for data in logger:
            if data["my.var"] > 10.0:
                break

    cf.set_param('my.param', '4711')
```

issue_title: Move Phantomjs to a single import and single instance
issue_body:
I'm pretty sure most of the reason that PhantomJS is slow is because we import and instantiate it every single time. A better solution would keep it around after it was started each time. An even better solution would keep it around, but only instantiate it when it was first needed (so calling just one scraper that doesn't use Phantomjs isn't negatively impacted).

There's [an answer on StackOverflow that has some tips about this][so]. This also might help with the Webdriver connection errors we seem to have on longer-running processes.

[so]: http://stackoverflow.com/a/27691489/64911

issue_title: Proposal: Bind deis containers to internal IPs
issue_body:
As deis assumes that cluster is connected to internal, private network, it should not expose its ports to the public.
For example, https://github.com/deis/deis/blob/master/deisctl/units/deis-database.service#L10. Here
```
ExecStart=/bin/sh -c "IMAGE=`/run/deis/bin/get_image /deis/database` && docker run --name deis-database --volumes-from=deis-database-data -p 5432:5432 -e EXTERNAL_PORT=5432 -e HOST=$COREOS_PRIVATE_IPV4 $IMAGE"
```
could be changed to something like 
```
ExecStart=/bin/sh -c "IMAGE=`/run/deis/bin/get_image /deis/database` && docker run --name deis-database --volumes-from=deis-database-data -p $COREOS_PRIVATE_IPV4:5432:5432 -e EXTERNAL_PORT=5432 -e HOST=$COREOS_PRIVATE_IPV4 $IMAGE"
```
This would not only make things more secure, as services like deis-database should not be accessed from outside, but would also allow to start attached services in the same cluster on usual ports.

issue_title: Applications with many web workers frequently fail healthchecks
issue_body:
I've been trying to setup healthchecks on an application with 12 web workers.  Almost every time I try to add the `HEALTHCHECK_URL` config, it fails because the healthcheck fails.  I've been visiting the healthcheck URL while this process is going on, and it has always reported success.

I looked into the deis-controller logs, and it seems to be failing like this:

    WARNING app: failed to connect to container ('Key not found : /deis/services/app/app.web.2')

We've been running a development version of this app with healthchecks for a couple of months without issue.  The main difference between the two versions is the number of workers they have - the production version having a lot more than the development version.

Could this be because the publisher is waiting for `healthcheck_initial_delay` seconds for each of the containers it's publishing in series, but the controller is only waiting once?  So if many of the containers end up on the same machine then the publisher will still be waiting for initial delays by the time the controller starts running it's healthchecks.

issue_title: Proposal feat(builder): allow custom slugrunner images
issue_body:
Using an env variable like `SLUGRUNNER_IMAGE` configured in the app (could be global too)

One reason for this feature is the reduction of the size in the final image. 
(I know that the application images have `heroku/cedarish as parent image but doing `docker save` from any app implies 1.2GB).

As an example, using:

```
FROM alpine:3.2

# Download and install glibc
RUN apk add --update curl bash libstdc++ openssl zlib && \
  curl -o glibc-2.21-r2.apk -sSL "https://circle-artifacts.com/gh/andyshinn/alpine-pkg-glibc/6/artifacts/0/home/ubuntu/alpine-pkg-glibc/packages/x86_64/glibc-2.21-r2.apk" && \
  apk add --allow-untrusted glibc-2.21-r2.apk && \
  curl -o glibc-bin-2.21-r2.apk -sSL  "https://circle-artifacts.com/gh/andyshinn/alpine-pkg-glibc/6/artifacts/0/home/ubuntu/alpine-pkg-glibc/packages/x86_64/glibc-bin-2.21-r2.apk" && \
  apk add --allow-untrusted glibc-bin-2.21-r2.apk && \
  /usr/glibc/usr/bin/ldconfig /lib /usr/glibc/usr/lib && \
  echo 'hosts: files mdns4_minimal [NOTFOUND=return] dns mdns4' >> /etc/nsswitch.conf && \
  rm -rf *.apk && \
  rm -rf /var/cache/apk/*

RUN mkdir /app

RUN addgroup -g 2000 slug && \
    adduser slug -D -u 2000 -G slug -h /app -H -s /bin/bash && \
    chown slug.slug /app

# add default port to expose (can be overridden)
ENV PORT 5000

EXPOSE 5000

ADD ./runner /runner

RUN chown slug:slug /runner/init

USER slug

ENV HOME /app

ENTRYPOINT ["/runner/init"]

ONBUILD WORKDIR /app

ONBUILD ADD slug.tgz /app
```

as docker slugrunner image is 17MB + the size of the application.

issue_title: Specifying conditional dependencies
issue_body:
Currently, wagon doesn't support packaging dependencies found under `extras_require` much more than wheel does: http://wheel.readthedocs.io/en/latest/#defining-conditional-dependencies

Of course, this can be easily worked around by adding an optional-requirements.txt file and passing it to wagon. Maybe we should add an `--include-extras` flag for that?

issue_title: conda update on packages that have both a hash and a non-hash build string for the latest version will cause an update on each successive run
issue_body:
## Current Behavior Running `conda update ` on packages that have both a hash and a non-hash build string for the latest version will cause an update on each successive run. Non-exhaustive list of packages for which this is a problem: - backports_abc - docutils - ipython_genutils - pickleshare - pygments - prompt_toolkit - traitlets - wcwidth ### Steps to Reproduce ``` $ conda create -y -n test python=2.7 $ conda activate test $ conda install docutils Solving environment: done ## Package Plan ## environment location: /home/gnash/miniconda/envs/test added / updated specs: - docutils The following NEW packages will be INSTALLED: docutils: 0.14-py27hae222c1_0 Proceed ([y]/n)? y Preparing transaction: done Verifying transaction: done Executing transaction: done $ conda update docutils Solving environment: done ## Package Plan ## environment location: /home/gnash/miniconda/envs/test added / updated specs: - docutils The following packages will be UPDATED: docutils: 0.14-py27hae222c1_0 --> 0.14-py27_0 Proceed ([y]/n)? y Preparing transaction: done Verifying transaction: done Executing transaction: done $ conda update docutils Solving environment: done ## Package Plan ## environment location: /home/gnash/miniconda/envs/test added / updated specs: - docutils The following packages will be UPDATED: docutils: 0.14-py27_0 --> 0.14-py27hae222c1_0 Proceed ([y]/n)? y Preparing transaction: done Verifying transaction: done Executing transaction: done ``` ## Expected Behavior I expect it to settle on a single version and not keep thinking that the not installed version is newer than the installed version. ## Environment Information
`conda info`

``` $ conda info active environment : test active env location : /home/gnash/miniconda/envs/test shell level : 1 user config file : /home/gnash/.condarc populated config files : conda version : 4.5.9 conda-build version : 3.12.1 python version : 2.7.11.final.0 base environment : /home/gnash/miniconda (writable) channel URLs : https://repo.anaconda.com/pkgs/main/linux-64 https://repo.anaconda.com/pkgs/main/noarch https://repo.anaconda.com/pkgs/free/linux-64 https://repo.anaconda.com/pkgs/free/noarch https://repo.anaconda.com/pkgs/r/linux-64 https://repo.anaconda.com/pkgs/r/noarch https://repo.anaconda.com/pkgs/pro/linux-64 https://repo.anaconda.com/pkgs/pro/noarch package cache : /home/gnash/miniconda/pkgs /home/gnash/.conda/pkgs envs directories : /home/gnash/miniconda/envs /home/gnash/.conda/envs platform : linux-64 user-agent : conda/4.5.9 requests/2.18.4 CPython/2.7.11 Linux/4.4.0-116-generic ubuntu/14.04 glibc/2.19 UID:GID : 10021:13227 netrc file : None offline mode : False ```

`conda config --show-sources`

``` $ conda config --show-sources ```

`conda list --show-channel-urls`

``` $ conda list --show-channel-urls # packages in environment at /home/gnash/miniconda/envs/test: # # Name Version Build Channel ca-certificates 2018.03.07 0 defaults certifi 2018.4.16 py27_0 defaults docutils 0.14 py27hae222c1_0 defaults libedit 3.1.20170329 h6b74fdf_2 defaults libffi 3.2.1 hd88cf55_4 defaults libgcc-ng 7.2.0 hdf63c60_3 defaults libstdcxx-ng 7.2.0 hdf63c60_3 defaults ncurses 6.1 hf484d3e_0 defaults openssl 1.0.2o h14c3975_1 defaults pip 10.0.1 py27_0 defaults python 2.7.15 h1571d57_0 defaults readline 7.0 ha6073c6_4 defaults setuptools 39.2.0 py27_0 defaults sqlite 3.24.0 h84994c4_0 defaults tk 8.6.7 hc745277_3 defaults wheel 0.31.1 py27_0 defaults zlib 1.2.11 ha838bed_2 defaults ```

issue_title: Use python-progressbar under the BSD license?
issue_body: The python-progressbar package is [dual licensed under both LGPL the BSD licenses](https://github.com/niltonvolpato/python-progressbar/blob/master/LICENSE.txt), including [back to the commit](https://github.com/niltonvolpato/python-progressbar/tree/cc8d9ebd1a9f43eb4d80297d2e6435748c053457) that looks to correspond to the version initially imported into Conda. To be consistent with the overall license of conda, can we switch to using the code under the terms of the BSD license?

issue_title: conda install --download-only feature
issue_body:
Right now we have `--dry-run`, but is there a way to actually download the `.tar.bz2` files into `pkgs` without installing them? If not, can there be? 

`--download-only` is one design with precedent, but also maybe as a separate subcommand, like `pip download`. The overarching goal is to get `pkgs` populated with all the dependencies necessary for an application and freezing it out into something akin to [a wheelhouse](https://pip.pypa.io/en/stable/reference/pip_wheel/#examples) for reproducible installs ([a la](https://www.paypal-engineering.com/2016/09/22/python-by-the-c-side/)). 

Thanks in advance!

issue_title: Support for keywords with implicit values
issue_body:
Over in the [mycli](https://github.com/dbcli/mycli) project, a recent poster raised the issue of MySQL config files that support keywords with implicit values. MySQL equates ``opt_name`` in a config file with specifying `--opt_name` on the command line. For example:
```
[mysqld]
skip-external-locking

[mysqldump]
quick
```
That is equivalent to setting `skip-external-locking` and `quick` to `True`. In a sense, it is functioning as a toggle or switch.

Would adding support for this be something the ConfigObj project would consider?

I took a glance at what it might take to implement this. The `_keyword` regular expression could be rewritten as:
```python
_keyword = re.compile(r'''^ # line start
    (\s*)                   # indentation
    (                       # keyword
        (?:".*?")|          # double quotes
        (?:'.*?')|          # single quotes
        (?:[^'"=].*?)       # no quotes
    )
    (?:\s*=\s*              # divider
    (.*))?                  # value (including list values and comments)
    $   # line end
    ''',
    re.VERBOSE)
```
This makes the divider and value portion optional. [When the groups are evaluated](https://github.com/DiffSK/configobj/blob/master/src/configobj/__init__.py#L1636), here are two example results:
```python
>>> line = '  quick=on'
>>> mat = _keyword.match(line)
>>> print('indent: {0}\nkey: {1}\nvalue: {2}'.format(*[repr(g) for g in mat.groups()]))
indent: '  '
key: 'quick'
value: 'on'
>>> line = '  quick'
>>> mat = _keyword.match(line)
>>> print('indent: {0}\nkey: {1}\nvalue: {2}'.format(*[repr(g) for g in mat.groups()]))
indent: '  '
key: 'quick'
value: None
```

Then a conditional could be added to equate a value of `None` with `True`. Comments, of course, would be trickier -- the above regular expression treats a comment as part of the key.

Anyways, do you think this feature is a reasonable one?

issue_title: Coverage and number of bases at position differs in pileup
issue_body:
Hi,

I tried using pysam to extract bases from an NGS dataset for a list of SNP positions.

Alignment of the reads was against hg19. Data is in bam format. If I open the bam file in IGV, it shows me 124 reads for a given position and a 65:35% distribution for G and A.

If I open the same bam file with pysam and run the following code for the same position (-1-corrected), it also reports 124 reads, but only 98 bases. There are no Ns or (apparently) low quality reads

```

chrom = "chr" + first_snp["Chromosome"]
pos = int(first_snp["Position (Hg19)"])-1 # Correct for zero based counting

    if pileupcolumn.pos == pos:

        for pileupread in pileupcolumn.pileups:
            thebase = pileupread.alignment.query_sequence[pileupread.query_position]
                if thebase == "A":
                    A+=1

issue_title: Let VUnit execute simulators which are inside a container or VM, from outside
issue_body:
Because VUnit requires the simulator to be available in the path, and executable names (vsim, vcom, etc.) are 'hardcoded', VUnit must be installed in the same docker image as the simulator. In a use case, I have two images, one with GHDL and a different one with QuestaSim, and VUnit is installed in both of them. Indeed, because I have versions with and without VUnit, there are four images. Each time VUnit is updated, two of them have to be rebuilt.

My proposal is to make VUnit execute simulators which are inside a container, from outside:

- Have a local python installation, but do not install simulators in the host.
  - This would allow you not to compile GHDL in each test, but still test multiple python versions with the latest GHDL in travis.
- Build a single VUnit docker image that can interact with simulator containers.

In the latter, I would have three images instead of four, and I would have to rebuild a single one. Note how this would scale with more simulators. Furthermore, if the VUnit docker image was generated in the vunit travis-ci flow, I would consume it as an alternative to `ghdl/ext:vunit`.

- Is it possible to prepend (not append) args to every vsim, vcom, ghdl call?
- Is it possible to hook commands (to create/kill temporal containers) before and after each 'task'? By 'task' I mean a sequence a commands to be executed in the same container.

A workaround would be to 'hack' it at system level. That is, write some man-in-the-middle shell scripts to get VUnit's calls and forward them to containers. However, it is not clean, because it is not straighforward to handle the lifecycle of tests as VUnit does.

> @kraigher (Gitter):
> @1138-4EB Regarding your use case about customizing the command for running vsim etc. it has some overlap with the use case of running a batch server farm with bsub if you are familiar with that.
> Anyway such things are easier to discuss in an issue rather than in this chat.

---

### Description

The `az appservice web config hostname add` command uses the `--name` parameter to indicate the hostname, and not the web app name. This causes the hostname value to pick up the default web app name, if one is set. Could we just rename `--webapp-name` to `--name`, and add a new parameter called `--hostname` (or equivalent)? That way `--name` is used throughout the `appservice web` group and supports defaults holistically.

issue_title: append blob upload ignores --if-none-match
issue_body:
### Description
```
az storage blob upload --type append --container-name "blocklist" \
        --name "domainsurls-index-append" --file uploadfile --if-none-match \*
```

Will upload the content (and append it) even if the blob already exists.
If the blob is not of type append then the upload will fail as expected if the blob already exists.

This also applies to using an explicit ETag version with both `--if-none-match` and `--if-match`.

issue_title: az acs kubernetes get-credentials --ssh-key-file does not override searching in .ssh for release 2.0.4
issue_body:
### Description
**Outline the issue here:**

When using a custom ssh key with the command line option --ssh-key-file for az acs kubernetes get-credentials if a file exists in .ssh in the users home dir the command fails with Authentication Failed.

Looking at debug for 

`az acs kubernetes get-credentials --resource-group=<rg> --name=<acsn> --ssh-key-file=<key_rsa> --debug`

results in the relevant output as follows:

```
...
paramiko.transport : kex engine KexGroup14 specified hash_algo <built-in function openssl_sha1>
paramiko.transport : Switch to new keys ...
paramiko.transport : Adding ssh-rsa host key for aitcacs-aitcpoc-59688e.westeurope.cloudapp.azure.com: <host key>
paramiko.transport : Trying discovered key <private key> in /root/.ssh/id_rsa
paramiko.transport : userauth is OK
paramiko.transport : Authentication (publickey) failed.
Authentication failed.
Traceback (most recent call last):
...
```

When using acs 2.0.3 the same command and section will result in

```
...
paramiko.transport : kex engine KexGroup14 specified hash_algo <built-in function openssl_sha1>

paramiko.transport : Authentication (publickey) successful!

The workaround is to use 2.0.3, install using 

`pip install azure-cli-acs==2.0.3`

sudo as required.

issue_title: Switch from user AWS credentials to IAM Role approach
issue_body:
We’re passing around and storing AWS keys and secrets in a bunch of places, and we should switch to an anonymous IAM role-based approach to make for more granular control.

Things we know now:
- EC2 instances launched in a given role offer temporary AWS credentials that can be used to talk to S3, etc.
- Boto likes to detect and use these credentials, so we can stop storing them in configuration files if we want
- AWS policies for S3 are basically known

Things we do not know:
- How or whether to write AWS policies for EC2 that allow for launch and termination of temporary instances, but prevent deletion of permanent instances
- How our unit tests will behave when AWS credentials switch to contextual / environmental variables

Things to do:
1) [ ] Start to use `configs` and `logs` directories in the main data bucket for running new instances under `openaddr-run-ec2-command`
2) [ ] Configure current instances and autoscale groups to use a new role
3) [ ] Transition away from explicit AWS credentials in config entirely

issue_title: computer-readable output when run on a CI
issue_body:
I'd really like to use pre-commit for running in a CI job, and output a json blob (or other serialized primitive format). I think that pre-commit is great for being used as a runner for a large set of simple code quality tests.
I'm already looking into doing the legwork on this, but I wanted to get your opinions on this. 

So far, the feature list I'm implementing/"requiring" is:
- --json-output=filepath flag - there's no sane way to head off all of the prints, so this would probably have to dump to a tempfile somewhere. 
- replace the return value of ints with an intermediate retval interface between hooks and pre-commit main - with backcompat, of course. That way, I can encapsulate all text outputs in a list[string] and pass them back to the caller. 
- update existing hooks with an intermediate print() replacement, and optional argument to use the intermediate retval.
- Some interesting pre-commit hooks that I need (and will contribute back, if possible).

I'm posting this before I start the bulk of my work, to make sure I'm not stepping on any toes. At the least, I can fork and maybe bring it back in? Please tear this request up.

Btw, I've already started using pre-commit for our needs in a prototype capacity, and I'm really happy with it.

issue_title: Print actual files being fixed by hooks
issue_body:
Hi,

First of all thanks for pre-commit, it's awesome!
While running isort as a hook, I noticed that it wasn't printing the files being fixed which isn't handy IMO. I could do a PR in isort but I thought it could be integrated in pre-commit. In fact, the current behaviour is that it prints "Files were modified by this hook. Additional output:" whenever a hook modifies a file, but it does not print the actual files.

I believe it could be useful to print the files being changed and not relying on the hook to do so. I think I can do that by parsing the output of "git diff --no-ext-diff" and extract filenames. issue_title: Python support for virtual environments
issue_body:
I make havy use of virtual environments, both for python 2 and pyton 3 project development.

Currently the 'default' behaviour seems to be to install the books in ~/.pre-commit with as python interpreter the one that is 'currently' active. So when installing hooks from a python3 virtual env. This hook will called with an python 3 interpreter, when installing another hook from python2, it will run, using python2.

As some hooks give different results (e.g. yapf) depending on the python interpreter used, this leads to 'strange' behaviour.

A solution could be to check for a virtual environment and if pre-commit is run inside such a setup, use the virtuel environment to install the hooks and run the hooks from.

This will stop wondering why sometimes files are reformatted, for no other reason than that they are 'processed' with a different interpreter.

issue_title: Strange behaviour when passing params
issue_body:
## Long story short

Doing something like this:

```py
async with session.get('https://httpbin.org/get', params={'test: 'foo;bar'}) as resp:
    print(await resp.json()['url'])
```

will print a strange URL.

## Expected behaviour

The URL should be `https://httpbin.org/get?test=foo%3Bbar`

## Actual behaviour

The URL is `https://httpbin.org/get?test=foo&bar=`

## Steps to reproduce

See above.

## Your environment

- OS: Ubuntu 16.04
- Python version: 3.6.1
- aiohttp version: 2.25

`requests` does what I expect:

```py
>>> import requests
>>> requests.get('https://httpbin.org/get', params={'test': 'foo;bar'})
<Response [200]>
>>> _.json()['url']
'https://httpbin.org/get?test=foo%3Bbar'
```

So I figure the current behaviour must be a bug.

issue_title: don't print full environment in error messages when starting a new kernel
issue_body:
We just had someone post an error log from starting up a kernel that had their full environment variables listed. This often can contain sensitive security-related information (like passwords, private tokens, etc.), so we shouldn't list all of their environment by default (maybe just their path, which is often relevant?). The problem is that people will try to be helpful and post their logs, not realizing that they contain sensitive information.

I think to reproduce this, modify a kernelspec so that it won't start, or points to a nonexistent file, and then try starting a new notebook. Look at the server logs for the "Failed to run command".

issue_title: [request] reconsider using xdg paths
issue_body:
https://github.com/ipython/ipython/issues/48
https://github.com/ipython/ipython/pull/4457
https://github.com/ipython/ipython/issues/6070
https://github.com/jupyter/notebook/issues/211

Sorry to beat this apparently dead horse, but I was testing out Jupyter and just realised that the config directory was wrong. And then with growing disbelief discovered that this was once reverted and even held onto.

The "often used in documentation" issue is cleanly solved by calling it [config dir] and linking to a page containing the paths listed by OS. This is standard procedure, don't tell me nobody with documentation commit access wants to take the time to create a single additional page and run `find doc/ -name "*.html" | xargs sed -i 's/\.jupyter/\<a href...\>\[config dir\]\<\/a\>/'"` (obviously run the adequate link syntax on the source of however you build your doc, possible mistakes should be trivially checkable with grep).

And unless the config directory on Windows is ~/.jupyter as well (haven't checked, but assume/hope it's not), this weird "config goes here" idea could be strictly improved by hard-coding it to Desktop/jupyter-config (or Desktop\jupyter-config, if you will) to be consistent across platforms. Then you don't have to ask users about their platform (or point them to the doc page) AND don't have to tell them to navigate a terminal / unhide files.

On a more serious note, going by the disussion so far there really seem to be no valid reasons to hold onto ~/.jupyter (`jupyter --paths` tells me config/data/runtime are separated nowadays), whereas OSX and Linux users would be thankful for the proper locations, as would those who have to deploy Jupyter in computer rooms... so here's another plea to reconsider a little bit before wontfixing this ;)

issue_title: Warn if message signing disabled
issue_body:
Certain kernels apparently haven't implemented message signing (e.g. zabirauf/icsharp#17), and in at least one case, a kernel installation routine writes a notebook config file to disable message signing so that it will work.

This can happen without users ever being aware that they're reducing their security. We should display a reasonably loud warning on starting the server if signing is disabled (maybe even something in the UI, rather than just the terminal), to give users a chance to notice this. It would also be useful to indicate to kernel authors that disabling message signing is for development only, and not something they should do on users' machines.

issue_title: Improving 4.x nbextensions
issue_body:
@fperez @damianavila @bollwyvl @sccolbert teoliphant @minrk @takluyver @ijstokes

I have been having a lot of on- and off-line discussion this week about the current state of nbextensions in 4.x. For a long time, we (at least this was my own logic) have hesitated to make any significant changes to how 4.x nbextensions are installed/loaded/packaged, because we know that much bigger changes are on the way in 5.0. After these recent conversations, I am convinced that we need to improve the existing 4.x nbextension architecture in the meantime. The current situation in 4.x is causing way to many problems for users and devs.

This goal of this issue is to 1) raise our community awareness that we need to do something about this and 2) come up with a concrete proposal for moving forward.

## Current pain points of 4.x nbextensions

nbextensions can be installed in the following locations:

```python
In [2]: paths.jupyter_path('nbextensions')
Out[2]: 
['/Users/bgranger/Library/Jupyter/nbextensions',
 '/Users/bgranger/anaconda/envs/python34/share/jupyter/nbextensions',
 '/usr/local/share/jupyter/nbextensions',
 '/usr/share/jupyter/nbextensions']
```

Config can be loaded from the following locations:

```python
In [4]: paths.jupyter_config_path()
Out[4]: 
['/Users/bgranger/.jupyter',
 '/Users/bgranger/anaconda/envs/python34/etc/jupyter',
 '/usr/local/etc/jupyter',
 '/etc/jupyter']
```

By default, installed extensions are not loaded, until activated. The **only** place extensions can be activated is in the *users* config directory (`~/.jupyter/nbconfig/notebook|notebook.json`):

```python
In [13]: cat /Users/bgranger/.jupyter/nbconfig/notebook.json
{
  "load_extensions": {
    "widgets/notebook/js/extension": true,
    "create_assignment/main": true,
    "nbgrader/create_assignment": true
  }
}
```

**Pain Point #1**: even though nbextensions and config can be installed in *system*, *sys.prefix* or *user* paths, the list of extensions to activate is only loaded from the *user* config. Thus, there is no way of activating nbextensions at the system or `sys.prefix` level.

* Example 1a: Because of this a JupyterHub deployment can't enable the various nbgrader extensions at the system level - each user has to do it. 
* Example 1b: Continuum can't enable extensions on a conda env basis, even though they can install them there.

**Point Point #2**: There is no standard package format for nbextensions (other than a directory of stuff) and no standard way of copying an nbextension into place. Because of this, there are multiple, separate hacky ways of packaging and installing nbextensions.

* Example 2a: nbgrader has created custom subcommands, such as `nbgrader extension install` and `nbgrader extensions activate`.
* Example 2b: Continuum is starting a couple of new projects to try to solve these issues in the conda context.
* Example 2c: We ourselves, just gave up and hardcoded the ipywidgets nbextensionss in the frontend code.
* Example 2d: Projects like https://github.com/takluyver/cite2c have a separate `install.py` script that installs and activate its extension.
* Example 2e: @minrk recommends just using `ls -n` to "install" his nbextensions here: https://github.com/minrk/ipython_extensions

## Proposal for addressing these pain points.

Here is the overall approach:

* Solve the above pain points in a full backwards compatible manner.
* Release the fixes quickly in a 4.2 release.
* In 5.x on the exiting pages we have (notebook/tree): formally deprecate the 4.x approach to loading/installing nbextensions, our existing JS APIs and CSS classes.
* In 5.x on the new JupyterLab page: only use new APIs and the new npm based plugin approach.

Here are the technical details of the proposal:

**Pain Point 1**

@takluyver has made an excellent point that the "nbconfig" frontend configuration system deliberately only loads from the users config (`~/.jupyter`) because these are really mean to only be "user preferences". I completely agree with this and others I have spoken to also concur. So we can't make that part of our app start to load system or `sys.prefix` based config.

I propose to start to load nbextension activation config from *all* config paths (system, `sys.prefix` and user) *always*, but do that instead by throwing that data into the `page.html` template itself, rather than loading later using the nbconfig web service. This would allow us to keep system data out of the user level app-preferences but still load config for nbextensions from all locations. This is not difficult to implement and is fully backwards compatible.

**Pain Point 2**

This one is more difficult and my proposal will be more controversial. Today, in many cases, people are shipping JS code in Python packages. For 5.0 we are going to stop doing that and embrace npm as our package format and manager, but that would require breaking changes in 4.x, so is not on the table.

I propose, that for 4.2+ we embrace putting nbextensions into Python packages:

* Don't standardize specifically where in the python package they are. This allows existing packages such as nbgrader to not have to change anything.
* Instead, 1) provide metadata in `setup.py` that gives the package relative paths of those assets and 2) provide that same metadata in `__init__.py` to enable runtime inspection. Something like this:

```python
>>> import nbgrader
>>> nbgrader._jupyter_nbextension_paths
['nbextensions/assignment_list', 'nbextensions/create_assigment']
```

* Using this simple convention, we could then improve the existing `jupyter nbextension` command line tool to work with python packages: `jupyter nbextension install nbgrader` and `jupyter nbextension enable nbgrader`. We could also include flags the target installation/config to the *system*, *sys.prefix* and *user* directories. This would again be fully backwards compatible.
* Using this simple convension, Continuum can build conda installers for nbextensions with almost no effort.

issue_title: @Strings parsed incorrectly if beginning with `{Example}`
issue_body:
The following two strings are incorrectly parsed:
```
@String{TODS = {{ACM} Transactions on Database Systems ({TODS})}}
```

This will incorrectly be parsed as
```
u'journal': u'ACM} Transactions on Database Systems ({TODS})',
```
I.e. the initial `{` is lost.

The same happens with trailing quotes:
```
@STRING{LWDA16 = {Proceedings of the Conference "Lernen, Wissen, Daten, Analysen"}}
```
will lose the trailing `"`. bibtexparser should only remove _at most one_ set of _matching_ quotes.

issue_title: Strict mode - validate every implementation against its spec?
issue_body:
As per pytest-dev/pytest#1821 @RonnyPfannschmidt has mentioned it's worth discussing if `pluggy.PluginManager` should have a *strict* mode where every single plugin which is loaded is verified against a spec instead of only performing this validation each time `PluginManager.check_pending()` is called.

In `pytest` `check_pending()` is only done once just prior to the `pytest_cmdline_main(config=config)` call meaning unverified hooks can be loaded any time afterwards.

This issue is to open discussion and brainstorming.

issue_title: Support for test methods
Not compatible with resultslog
issue_body:
Hi,

The plugin is not compatible with the resultslog module from pytest.

By default the resultslog will not be created. To turn on this feature you need to set the `--resultlog` or `--result-log` pytest command line parameter. If we have a failing test and run pytest like so:

`$ pytest --result-log=pytest.log --reruns=3`

we will get the following error:

`...`
`INTERNALERROR>     res = hook_impl.function(*args)`
`INTERNALERROR>   File "c:\users\eostr\envs\pytest\lib\site-packages\_pytest\resultlog.py", line 86, in pytest_runtest_logreport`
`INTERNALERROR>     self.log_outcome(report, code, longrepr)`
`INTERNALERROR> UnboundLocalError: local variable 'longrepr' referenced before assignment`

This problem is cause because when a test fails for the first time (and not last time) line 86 is executed. The value of `report` will be set to `rerun`. In `pytest.pytest_runtest_logreport` there are a bunch of check that set the value of `longrepr` based on `report`. Since `rerun` is not valid value, `longrepr` does not get set.

Cheers,
Edgar

issue_title: Devepers need a single source of truth to see list of relevant design patterns
Non scalar values for sigma0?
issue_body:
I am curious as to why only scalar values for sigma0 are allowed. It seems that there shouldnt be any reason why we cannot not have a vector of sigma0 values for each dimension of the optimization problem.

In fact this is possible in the java implementation of CMA-ES: 

https://commons.apache.org/proper/commons-math/javadocs/api-3.1/org/apache/commons/math3/optim/nonlinear/scalar/noderiv/CMAESOptimizer.Sigma.html

issue_title: Fix #1638 Will Brake Orders Placed and Offer Performance Reports
issue_body:
Note: accepting my fix for #1638 will break two of the reports if you attempt to generate them without a date range specified. (Technically, they are already broken)

I believe this is because these two reports incorrectly use their custom `generate()` method to filter via dates instead of the more robust `ReportGenerator.filter_with_date_range()`.

I will attempt to create a pull request for these two reports tomorrow.

Reports:
1. https://github.com/django-oscar/django-oscar/blob/1.0.1/oscar/apps/order/reports.py#L54
2. https://github.com/django-oscar/django-oscar/blob/1.0.1/oscar/apps/offer/reports.py#L45

issue_title: Error when running "docker run" .net core 2.0 preview project in linux
issue_body:
I'm upgrading our project to .net core 2.0 preview now. 

Our project now uses .net core 2.0 preview, in Docker container, pushed to Microsoft azure container service with orchestration of kubernetes.

Package being used in project:
`<PackageReference Include="Microsoft.AspNetCore.All" Version="2.0.0-preview2-final" />`

dotnet runtime in (VSTS) build agent machine,
```
$ dotnet --version
2.0.0-preview2-006497
```

SDKs installed in the machine,
```
$ ls /usr/share/dotnet/shared/Microsoft.NETCore.App
1.0.5  1.1.2  2.0.0-preview2-25407-01
```
DockerFile: [reference](https://hub.docker.com/r/microsoft/aspnetcore/)

```
FROM microsoft/dotnet:2.0.0-runtime-jessie

# set up network
ENV ASPNETCORE_URLS http://+:80
ENV ASPNETCORE_RUNTIMESTORE_DOWNLOAD_URL https://dist.asp.net/runtimestore/2.0.0/linux-x64/aspnetcore.runtimestore.tar.gz
ENV ASPNETCORE_PKG_VERSION 2.0.0

# set up the runtime store
RUN curl -o /tmp/runtimestore.tar.gz $ASPNETCORE_RUNTIMESTORE_DOWNLOAD_URL \
    && export DOTNET_HOME=$(dirname $(readlink $(which dotnet))) \
    && tar -x -C $DOTNET_HOME -f /tmp/runtimestore.tar.gz \
    && rm /tmp/runtimestore.tar.gz

ARG source
WORKDIR /app
EXPOSE 80
COPY ${source:-obj/Docker/publish/References.Web} .

ENTRYPOINT ["dotnet", "References.Web.dll"]
```

I tried some other docker images, yet, all have the below issue when running the container,

```
It was not possible to find any compatible framework version 
The specified framework 'Microsoft.NETCore.App', version '2.0.0-preview2-25407-01' was not found. 
  - Check application dependencies and target a framework version installed at: 
      / 
  - Alternatively, install the framework version '2.0.0-preview2-25407-01'. 
```

Thanks

Created an issue in aspnet-docker project 
https://github.com/aspnet/aspnet-docker/issues/281

issue_title: Python Test Script
issue_body:
Python test scripts for developers, should cover all library features.

Dev needs to enter test credentials locally, otherwise all tests fail with missing credentials and tests won't be of much use.

To avoid having credentials in source code, maybe it's useful to add function to load credentials from JSON file? This way a path can be given to credentials file which can easily be added to .gitignore.

issue_title: UNTIL time does not unclude Z (Zulu) ending
issue_body:
I'm using python dateutils 2.7.3 and having a problem with an rrule UNTIL date.

The __str__() method specifically omits the timezone: https://github.com/dateutil/dateutil/blob/2.7.3/dateutil/rrule.py#L717

Which outputs a string like: "RRULE:FREQ=DAILY;UNTIL=20180827T170000"

instead of: "RRULE:FREQ=DAILY;UNTIL=20180827T170000Z"

RFC5545 says UTC is required, which makes me think rrule.__str__() should be appending the Z itself.

Why is it not? Am I using it incorrectly?

My ultimate goal is to write recurring events to the google calendar API. But google API rejects the date without the Z, which makes sense.

issue_title: CommandLineTool.arguments: string interpolation with `$(self)` incorrectly fails
issue_labels: [{'id': 269875289, 'node_id': 'MDU6TGFiZWwyNjk4NzUyODk=', 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/common-workflow-language/cwltool/labels/bug', 'name': 'bug', 'color': 'fc2929', 'default': True}, {'id': 832475409, 'node_id': 'MDU6TGFiZWw4MzI0NzU0MDk=', 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/common-workflow-language/cwltool/labels/good%20first%20issue', 'name': 'good first issue', 'color': 'd4c5f9', 'default': True}, {'id': 269875292, 'node_id': 'MDU6TGFiZWwyNjk4NzUyOTI=', 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/common-workflow-language/cwltool/labels/help%20wanted', 'name': 'help wanted', 'color': '159818', 'default': True}]
issue_body:
According to https://www.commonwl.org/v1.0/CommandLineTool.html#Parameter_references
If a parameter reference is allowed in a field, then `self` is allowed; it should be `null` if not specifically defined.

`self-dump.cwl`
``` cwl
#!/usr/bin/env cwl-runner
cwlVersion: v1.0
class: CommandLineTool
inputs:
  a_string:
    type: string
    default: z
baseCommand: echo
arguments:
 - '{"self":$(self)}'
stdout: self.json
outputs:
  self_dump: stdout
```

```
$ cwltool self-dump.cwl 
/home/mcrusoe/cwltool/env3/bin/cwltool 1.0.20180528123027
Resolved 'self-dump.cwl' to 'file:///home/mcrusoe/cwltool/self-dump.cwl'
Workflow error, try again with --debug for more information:
self-dump.cwl:10:4: Expression evaluation error:
                    Syntax error in parameter reference 'self': self is not defined. This could be
                    due to using Javascript code without specifying InlineJavascriptRequirement.
```
Expected output: that `self.json` would contain `{"self": null}`

issue_title: Any[] is incompatible with any array type apart from Any[]
issue_body:
With the workflow:
```
cwlVersion: v1.0
class: Workflow

inputs:
  input:
    type: Any[]

steps: []

outputs:
  - id: output
    type: string[]
    outputSource: input
```
`cwltool --validate` reports the error:
```
/usr/local/bin/cwltool 1.0.20180130110340
Resolved 'workflows/test_workflow.cwl' to 'file:///Users/th10/checkouts/arvados-pipeline/arvados-pipelines/cwl/workflows/test_workflow.cwl'
Tool definition failed validation:
workflows/test_workflow.cwl:6:5: Source 'input' of type {"type": "array", "items": "Any"} is incompatible
workflows/test_workflow.cwl:12:5:   with sink 'output' of type {"type": "array", "items": "string"}
```

Any is supposed to be compatible with any other type, so Any[] should be compatible with any other array type - this is not the case.

## Your Environment
* cwltool version: 1.0.20180130110340

issue_title: Parameter with a valueFrom field, type array and no prefix doesn't lead to execution of the expected command
issue_body:
If you use the valueFrom field with an argument of array type and no prefix field, the executed command is the evaluated valueFrom field followed by the array arguments, not just the  evaluated valueFrom field. This is not what I would expect from the cwl specification and behaviour of cwltool.

## Expected Behaviour

From http://www.commonwl.org/v1.0/CommandLineTool.html#CommandLineBinding:
> If valueFrom is a constant string value, use this as the value and apply the binding rules above.

It is not specified in the specification what the binding rules should be if no prefix is defined, but in cwltool if no `prefix` field is present, then cwltool doesn't evaulate the anything for that argument e.g.:
```yaml
cwlVersion: v1.0
inputs:
  - id: parameter
    type:
      - type: array
        items: string
outputs: []
class: CommandLineTool
baseCommand: echo
```
```yaml
parameter:
  - "item1"
  - "item2"
```

Outputs:
```
/usr/local/bin/cwltool 1.0.20171107133715
Resolved 'test.cwl' to '...'
[job test.cwl] /private/var/folders/5p/p6z8hhy174q_ctt3pn7mx5_c000h1t/T/tmp1mivk5pg$ echo

[job test.cwl] completed success
{}
Final process status is success
```
I would therefore infer that using the workflow and inputs below should execute ` 'echo' 'replacementValue'`, but cwltool executes: `'echo' 'replacementValue' 'item1' 'item2'`.
## Actual Behaviour
Using the workflow and inputs below, cwltool outputs as specified below:

## Workflow Code
### Workflow
```yaml
cwlVersion: v1.0
requirements:
- class: ShellCommandRequirement
- class: InlineJavascriptRequirement
inputs:
  - id: parameter
    type:
      - type: array
        items: string
    inputBinding:
      valueFrom: replacementValue
outputs: []
class: CommandLineTool
baseCommand: echo
```
### Inputs
```yaml
parameter:
  - "item1"
  - "item2"
```

## Full Traceback
```pytb
/usr/local/bin/cwltool 1.0.20171107133715
Resolved 'test.cwl' to '...'
[job test.cwl] /private/var/folders/5p/p6z8hhy174q_ctt3pn7mx5_c000h1t/T/tmp_jdgzuc4$ /bin/sh \
    -c \
    'echo' 'replacementValue' 'item1' 'item2'
replacementValue item1 item2
[job intervals_test.cwl] completed success
{}
Final process status is success
```

## Your Environment
* cwltool version: 1.0.20171107133715

issue_title: Basic feature list - GeoJSON conversion
issue_body:
In light of #169 , I think it's important to establish a list of core features that folium is able to support and should provide in the API.

Following Leaflet, it seems the fundamental types should be:

- [x] Marker
- [ ] Path: abstract
- [x] Polyline: this needs name change to Polyline
- [ ] MultiPolyline
- [ ] Polygon: at the moment we only have RegularPolygon
- [ ] MultiPolygon
- [ ] Rectangle
- [ ] Circle: not sure if we should include this.
- [x] CircleMarker.

We should also support:
- [ ] LayerGroup
- [x] FeatureGroup
- [ ] GeoJSON: I would say this is half done.

I think that everything else, like divicons ( #169 ) is only style-oriented and can't have it's own method.

Also, two thoughts for the feature:
- we might convert each of these feature to a class and have a generic folium.add_feature, to keep code a bit tidier.
- we might associate to each feature a Geojson representation. This would make straightforward to output the *data* associated to a map and make it visualisable in other platform (e.g. GitHub)

issue_title: Factor out of folium what's not map specific.
issue_body:
Based on the discussion we've had with @ocefpaf @themiurgo @andrewgiessel in #300, it worth factoring a piece of the code out of folium, because it's not map-specific.

2 questions remain open then:

* What has to be externalized ? At least a bit of `colormap.py` but maybe also `element.py` and so on.

* Who / where will it be maintained ? In *python-visualization* organization, or another repo ? If another, which one ?

issue_title: Ensure that c. coordinate is within CDS bounds
issue_body:
We don't verify that a c. variant is within CDS bounds. This causes bugs in (at least) c_to_p. An example is #425.

At a minimum, we should add bounds checking to the ExtrinsicValidator class.

However, EV isn't invoked during projection because it's a bit expensive. One option is to start running EV by default, but also provide a way for callers to disable validation (i.e., enable them to assert that the variants have already been validated).

issue_title: Nested field with many=True and required=True gives unexpected error for missing data
issue_body:
Say I have following schema definition

```python
from marshmallow import Schema, fields


class ObjectSchema(Schema):
    id = fields.Integer(required=True)
    name = fields.String(required=True)


class CollectionSchema(Schema):
    data = fields.Nested(ObjectSchema, many=True, required=True)
```

Here's how this schema works for several invalid inputs

```
In [6]: CollectionSchema().load({})
Out[6]: UnmarshalResult(data={}, errors={'data': {0: {'name': ['Missing data for required field.'], 'id': ['Missing data for required field.']}}})

In [7]: CollectionSchema().load({'data': []})
Out[7]: UnmarshalResult(data={'data': []}, errors={})

In [8]: CollectionSchema().load({'data': [{}]})
Out[8]: UnmarshalResult(data={'data': [{}]}, errors={'data': {0: {'name': ['Missing data for required field.'], 'id': ['Missing data for required field.']}}})
```

IMO `Out[8]` is the only correct validation error from the examples - it correctly describes the problem - all required fields are missing in nested item. For other examples I actually expect something like:

1\. 
```
In [6]: CollectionSchema().load({})
Out[6]: UnmarshalResult(data={}, errors={'data': ['Missing data for required field.']})
```
shows that 'data' field is required and missing. I'm missing this one badly.

2\.
```
In [7]: CollectionSchema().load({'data': []})
Out[7]: UnmarshalResult(data={'data': []}, errors={'data': ['Field may not be empty list.']})
```
shows that field contains empty list - for my usecase it's the same as missing field. Well, this one is arguable, maybe `Nested` field requires another parameter to enforce validation errors in case of empty nested value.

So currently marshmallow cannot distinguish between these three cases which complicates automated error handling in JSON api I'm working on now - as I said errors do not describe the problem properly. What do you think - is it an issue or I'm missing something and there's better approach to this problem?

class CourseSchema(rod.model.BaseSchema):

        model = rod.model.course.Course

    levels = marshmallow_sqlalchemy.field_for(rod.model.course.Course, 'levels', dump_only=True)
```

Loading the object:
```python
data = {
    'title': 'Test Course',
    'levels': [1, 2, 3]  # Should be ignored
}
course = CourseSchema().load(data).data
    
print course.levels
```

Expected:

None

Actual:

{InstrumentedList} [<Level object>, <Level object>, <Level object>]
```

issue_title: Use of WARP for display adverstising
issue_body:
Hi Maciej,

I'm here not for an issue but to have your advice on the following. Please bear with me.
I work in the re-marketing domain of Inmobi, a mobile ad-network. I am trying to solve recommendation problem for cold-start users. I wish to use lightFM, which I think is a really great project you have released in open-source.

Historical data I have: 
- supply+context+user features; eg: publisher, ad slot, day of week, user handset details, user's derived features etc. Hence forth these features will be referred to as Uf
- demand features ie features of items whose ads were shown; eg: brand, category, price, discount etc. Hence forth these feature will be referred to as Vf
- metric: CTR (clicks/impressions shown)
- Hence the data format is: Uf*Vf matrix of partially filled CTR values. 
- Note that user ids are not available. Only features are available.

Objective: 
- Show the most relevant product to the user. Prediction at user level is difficult due to latency issues. Hence Uf represent user type and Vf act as item type. Thus the refined objective is: show the most relevant item type to a user type. Traditional methods used are CTR prediction using classification models. 
- Once the best item type is selected for a given user type, we can choose ads of most popular items in that item type cohort to show to this user type. 

Two approaches I have thought of:
1. Use implicit matrix factorization using ALS/SGD, where implicit score = CTR and confidence = #impressions. In my understanding, this will give results similar to traditional classification methods. Eg: http://blog.ethanrosenthal.com/2016/10/19/implicit-mf-part-1/
2. Learning to rank using WARP loss. Intuitively this should given better results as we are concerned with only top k recommendations for each user type. eg: http://blog.ethanrosenthal.com/2016/11/07/implicit-mf-part-2/

Question I am struggling with, while trying to use lightFM:
1. Are there any flaws in above approaches? 
2. In the 2nd approach, how do I model the CTR matrix into implicit preference matrix? Is there something like "confidence" which can be incorporated? (just like in 1st approach)
3. My data doesn't have user id. Can I still employ any of the above approaches using lightFM? In my understanding, I can feed above Uf*Vf matrix as user*item matrix input to lightFM package and that should be fine?

It will be great if you guide me w.r.t. the above approaches and questions. Any other suggestion is most welcome as well.

Thank You 
Kunal Kishore

issue_title: predict_ranks returns non-unique ranks when test and train have samples in common
issue_body:
Not sure if you consider this an issue but, if data source contains duplicates (and the user does not remove them) they might get into both train and test set. This would be bad enough, but an additional problem with that is that when running `predict_ranks` and supplying `train_interaction` to ignore, the returning ranks are non unique (e.g `[0, 0, 0, 1, 1, ...]`), which makes evaluation even more optimistic (on top of being methodically incorrect because of duplicates). 
Although it's the user's fault, the ranks shouldn't be non-unique (and optimistic). In any case, both problems may be solved by raising an error or a warning if an intersection of test and train is detected in `predict_ranks`. Would you accept a PR for that?

Code to reproduce:

```
import scipy.sparse as sp
import numpy as np

from lightfm import LightFM
lfm = LightFM()

train_mat = sp.csr_matrix(np.concatenate([np.ones((6,3)), np.zeros((6,3))], axis=1))
print(train_mat.toarray())

test_mat = sp.csr_matrix(np.concatenate([np.zeros((6,2)), np.ones((6,4))], axis=1))
print(test_mat.toarray())

lfm = lfm.fit(train_mat)

ranks = lfm.predict_rank(test_mat)
# without training exclusion ranks are unique
print(ranks.tolil().data)

# when trying to exclude training ranks have duplicates
ranks_no_train = lfm.predict_rank(test_mat, train_interactions=train_mat)
print(ranks_no_train.tolil().data)
```

issue_title: simplify the clearsky.ineichen function
issue_body:
I think that clearsky.ineichen is a disaster of a function. There's some unnecessary complexity inherited from PVLIB Matlab, and some additional complexity that I wrongly added.

In the spirit of simple functions, I think we should:
* remove the ``time, latitude, location`` arguments in favor of ``apparent_zenith``
* make ``airmass`` a required argument
* remove the ability for the function to calculate it's own solar position and airmass
* remove the ability for the function to look up linke turbidity

The ``Location`` class's ``get_clearsky`` method, or similar, can provide the higher-level features.

My simplified solis PR #148 might provide some inspiration.

issue_title: QUESTION! Step-like changes in plane-of-array irradiance gradients
issue_body:
I was trying to model how solar geometry and plane-of-array irradiance at noon change through the year.
I'd picked Hawaii for the modeling because they don't observe daylight saving.

Here is my code (based on [LocalizedPVSystem](https://pvlib-python.readthedocs.io/en/latest/package_overview.html#object-oriented-localizedpvsystem) section of pvlib-python.readthedocs.io documentation)

```python
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np

import pvlib
from pvlib.pvsystem import LocalizedPVSystem

temp_air = 20
wind_speed = 0

mdl = pvlib.pvsystem.retrieve_sam('SandiaMod')['Canadian_Solar_CS5P_220M___2009_']
inv = pvlib.pvsystem.retrieve_sam('cecinverter')['ABB__MICRO_0_25_I_OUTD_US_208_208V__CEC_2014_']
 
localized_system = LocalizedPVSystem(
    module_parameters=mdl,
    inverter_parameters=inv,
    surface_tilt=20,
    surface_azimuth=180,
    latitude=19.5,
    longitude=156,
    altitude=14,
    name='KOA', # airport code
    tz='US/Hawaii'
)

times = pd.DatetimeIndex(start='2015-01-01T12:00:00', end='2016-01-01T12:00:00', freq='1d')
times = times.tz_localize(localized_system.tz)

clearsky = localized_system.get_clearsky(times)

solar_position = localized_system.get_solarposition(times)

total_irrad = localized_system.get_irradiance(
    solar_position['apparent_zenith'],
    solar_position['azimuth'],
    clearsky['dni'], clearsky['ghi'], clearsky['dhi'],
    model='perez'
)
```

Then I had noticed that modeled PoA curves are not completely smooth and calculated their pseudo-gradients using [pandas.DataFrame.diff](https://pandas.pydata.org/pandas-docs/stable/generated/pandas.DataFrame.diff.html) method

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/16244350/29918352-d7fa4d20-8e4d-11e7-980f-28df864b0e40.png)

Then I calculated pseudo-gradients for the solar geometry and there are no step-like changes in gradients

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/16244350/29918409-1359daca-8e4e-11e7-973c-715a1ed8d619.png)

I've tried several other locations, module orientations and 'model' argument values having similar step-like changes.

**The question is:** Is it okay that gradual changes in solar position causes step-like changes in the plane-of-array irradiation? It seems unnatural to me.

Many thanks in advance!

P.S. I'm volunteering to contribute to the fixing process if this behavior is an issue.

issue_title: Incorrect model-value with recursive functions
issue_body:
For this script:

```
(declare-fun items ()  (Array Int Int))

(assert (= (select items 1) 4))
(assert (= (select items 2) 4))

(define-fun-rec computeSize ((lb Int) (ub Int)) Int
   (ite (> lb ub)
        0
        (+ (select items lb)
           (computeSize (+ lb 1) ub))))

(define-fun totalSize () Int (computeSize 1 2))
(assert (> totalSize 1))

(check-sat)
(get-value (totalSize))
```

Z3 produces:

```
sat
((totalSize 0))
```

which is clearly wrong, as the result violates the assertion `(> totalSize 1)`. Should compute to `8` in any case.
